  • Worktree Advanced Patterns -- Multi-worktree workflows and team collaboration
  • Setup Parallel Worktrees -- Work on multiple features simultaneously

docs.title: Recipe: Deploy Course Website
docs.text: Time: 3 minutes Level: Beginner Prerequisites: Teaching mode detected (course site with Quarto/MkDocs/pkgdown)

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I want to safely publish course website updates to GitHub Pages without breaking existing content.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Preview changes locally
/craft:site:build

Why: Builds the site locally so you can review changes before publishing

  1. Check for issues
/craft:site:check

Why: Validates links, images, and common issues that could break the site

  1. Deploy to GitHub Pages
/craft:site:deploy

Why: Publishes to gh-pages branch with automatic backup and rollback support

  1. Verify deployment
  2. Check the deployment URL (shown in output)
  3. Verify all pages load correctly
  4. Test navigation and links

Flow-CLI Config Compatibility

If your project uses flow-cli's teach-config.yml (with semester_info, course.name, branches), Craft reads it natively — no separate config needed. See Config Schema for details.

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
/craft:site:deploy implements a safe deployment workflow:

  1. Pre-deployment checks:
  2. Validates all internal links are not broken
  3. Checks that build succeeds without errors

  4. Verifies git working directory is clean (no uncommitted changes)

  5. Backup current state:

  6. Creates a backup tag: backup-gh-pages-YYYY-MM-DD-HHMMSS

  7. Allows quick rollback if deployment fails

  8. Build and deploy:

  9. Detects site type (Quarto, MkDocs, pkgdown)
  10. Runs appropriate build command

  11. Pushes to gh-pages branch with force-with-lease (safe force push)

  12. Post-deployment verification:

  13. Checks that GitHub Pages build succeeds
  14. Provides deployment URL

  15. Suggests verification steps

  16. Rollback on failure:

  17. If deployment fails, automatically reverts to backup
  18. Shows rollback instructions

docs.title: Variations
docs.text:
• Preview without deploying:
/craft:site:build && /craft:site:check

Use when you want to review changes but not publish yet

  • Deploy with custom message:
/craft:site:deploy "Update Week 3 materials"

Adds a descriptive commit message to gh-pages branch

  • Skip validation (not recommended):
/craft:site:deploy --skip-checks

Use only if you're confident the site is valid and need to deploy urgently

  • Deploy to custom branch:
/craft:site:deploy --branch docs

Use if your GitHub Pages serves from a branch other than gh-pages

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "Broken links detected" Run /craft:site:check to see which links are broken, fix them, then retry "Working directory not clean" Commit or stash your changes before deploying "Build failed" Check build output for errors, fix issues, then retry "Permission denied (GitHub Pages)" Verify repository settings: Settings → Pages → Source is set to gh-pages branch "404 after deployment" GitHub Pages can take 1-2 minutes to update; wait and refresh "Old content still showing" Clear browser cache or try incognito mode

docs.title: Recipe: Find the Right Command
docs.text: Time: 2 minutes Level: Beginner Prerequisites: Craft installed

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I want to find the right Craft command for a task without memorizing all 108 commands.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Open the Hub
/craft:hub

Why: The Hub is a zero-maintenance command directory that auto-discovers all available commands

  1. Browse categories

The Hub shows top-level categories first:

Available Categories:
  arch      Architecture analysis (12 commands)
  ci        CI/CD automation (6 commands)
  code      Code quality (8 commands)
  docs      Documentation (14 commands)
  git       Git workflow (9 commands)
  site      Website management (6 commands)
  test      Testing (10 commands)
  workflow  Workflow tools (5 commands)

Why: Progressive disclosure keeps the list manageable instead of showing all 108 commands at once

  1. Drill into a category
/craft:hub code

Why: Shows just the commands in that category with short descriptions

Code Commands:
  code:lint       Run code quality checks
  code:format     Auto-format code files
  code:coverage   Generate coverage report
  code:ci-local   Run CI checks locally
  ...
  1. View command details
/craft:hub code:lint

Why: Shows full usage, flags, examples, and execution modes for that specific command

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: The Hub uses 3-layer progressive disclosure: Main (all categories) then Category (commands in one group) then Detail (full docs for one command). It reads command metadata directly from YAML frontmatter in the command files, so new commands appear automatically without any manual registry updates.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: You have a project (plugin, CLI tool, ZSH plugin, or MCP server) and want a comprehensive test suite generated automatically.

docs.title: 1. Auto-detect and generate
docs.text:
/craft:test:gen

The generator detects your project type from indicator files and renders Jinja2 templates into test files.

docs.title: 2. Preview first (recommended)
docs.text:
/craft:test:gen --dry-run

Output:

Test Generation Plan
Project: my-plugin (plugin)
Template: templates/plugin/
Output: tests/

Files to generate:
  [new]    tests/test_structure.py     (7 tests)
  [new]    tests/test_commands.py      (24 tests)
  [new]    tests/test_skills.py        (5 tests)
  [skip]   tests/conftest.py           (already exists)

docs.title: 3. Run generated tests
docs.text: /craft:test unit

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The generator works in 4 steps:

  1. Detect — Checks for indicator files (.claude-plugin/plugin.json, *.plugin.zsh, etc.)
  2. Gather — Scans the project for variables (command names, skills, entry points)
  3. Render — Applies Jinja2 templates with gathered variables
  4. Write — Creates test files in tests/ (skips existing files unless --force)

docs.title: Force a project type
docs.text:
/craft:test:gen plugin
/craft:test:gen cli
/craft:test:gen mcp

docs.title: Generate only unit tests
docs.text: /craft:test:gen --tier unit

docs.title: Overwrite existing tests
docs.text: /craft:test:gen --force

docs.title: Generate to a different directory
docs.text: /craft:test:gen --output my-tests/

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Fix "Could not detect project type" Use --type to force detection "Template render error" Run /craft:test:template validate Tests fail after generation Generated tests check real structure — fix the issues they find jinja2 not installed pip install jinja2

docs.title: Recipe: Post-Merge Documentation Update
docs.text: Time: 3-5 minutes Level: Beginner Prerequisites: Just merged a feature branch to dev/main NEW: v2.9.0 - Automated 5-phase documentation pipeline

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I just merged a feature branch and need to update documentation to reflect the changes. I want an automated workflow that catches stale docs, broken links, and missing updates.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Run post-merge documentation update (v2.9.0)
/craft:docs:update --post-merge

What happens (v2.9.0 automated pipeline):

╭─ Post-Merge Documentation Pipeline ──────────╮
│ Analyzing merged changes...                   │
│ Detected: 8 files changed in last merge       │
│                                               │
│ Running 5-phase pipeline:                     │
│ 1. Detect stale documentation                 │
│ 2. Validate internal links                    │
│ 3. Auto-fix safe issues                       │
│ 4. Generate update recommendations            │
│ 5. Rebuild documentation site                 │
│                                               │
│ Starting phase 1...                           │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯
  1. Review detected issues (Phase 1)

The pipeline analyzes your merge and reports:

╭─ Phase 1: Stale Documentation Detected ──────╮
│                                               │
│ Found 3 potentially stale documents:          │
│                                               │
│ 1. docs/commands/check.md                     │
│    Reason: /commands/check.md changed         │
│    Recommendation: Update command examples    │
│                                               │
│ 2. docs/reference/REFCARD.md                  │
│    Reason: New flags added to check command   │
│    Recommendation: Add --post-merge to flags  │
│                                               │
│ 3. CLAUDE.md                                  │
│    Reason: Version likely outdated            │
│    Recommendation: Sync version number        │
│                                               │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Continue to phase 2? (y/n)
  1. Validate links (Phase 2)

Automatically checks all documentation links:

╭─ Phase 2: Link Validation ───────────────────╮
│                                               │
│ Checking 1,247 links across 94 files...      │
│                                               │
│ Results:                                      │
│ ✓ 1,243 links valid                           │
│ ✗ 4 broken links found:                       │
│                                               │
│ 1. docs/guide/old-tutorial.md                 │
│    → docs/tutorials/new-tutorial.md (moved)   │
│                                               │
│ 2. commands/deprecated.md                     │
│    → File deleted in merge                    │
│                                               │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Continue to phase 3? (y/n/fix-manually)
  1. Auto-fix safe issues (Phase 3)

The pipeline automatically fixes what it can:

╭─ Phase 3: Auto-Fix ──────────────────────────╮
│                                               │
│ Fixing safe issues:                           │
│                                               │
│ ✓ Updated 2 redirected links                  │
│ ✓ Removed 1 link to deleted file              │
│ ✓ Fixed 3 version numbers in CLAUDE.md        │
│ ✓ Updated navigation in mkdocs.yml            │
│                                               │
│ Manual intervention needed:                   │
│ • docs/commands/check.md (content update)     │
│   Action: Add examples for --post-merge flag  │
│                                               │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Continue to phase 4? (y/n)
  1. Review recommendations (Phase 4)

Guidance for manual updates:

╭─ Phase 4: Update Recommendations ────────────╮
│                                               │
│ Priority updates needed:                      │
│                                               │
│ HIGH PRIORITY:                                │
│ • docs/commands/check.md                      │
│   Add: --post-merge flag documentation        │
│   Add: Example of post-merge workflow         │
│   Estimated: 5-10 minutes                     │
│                                               │
│ MEDIUM PRIORITY:                              │
│ • docs/tutorials/                             │
│   Consider: Tutorial for post-merge pipeline  │
│   Estimated: 30 minutes                       │
│                                               │
│ LOW PRIORITY:                                 │
│ • docs/changelog.md                           │
│   Add: Entry for v2.9.0 post-merge feature    │
│   Estimated: 2 minutes                        │
│                                               │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Update now or defer? (update/defer/list)
  1. Rebuild and verify (Phase 5)

Final phase rebuilds documentation:

╭─ Phase 5: Rebuild Documentation Site ────────╮
│                                               │
│ Building with mkdocs...                       │
│ ✓ Build successful (3.2s)                     │
│ ✓ All pages generated                         │
│ ✓ No build warnings                           │
│                                               │
│ Verification:                                 │
│ ✓ Site builds without errors                  │
│ ✓ Navigation structure valid                  │
│ ✓ Search index generated                      │
│                                               │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

╭─ Pipeline Complete ──────────────────────────╮
│ Documentation updated successfully!           │
│                                               │
│ Auto-fixed: 6 issues                          │
│ Manual updates recommended: 1                 │
│ Site rebuilt: 94 pages                        │
│                                               │
│ Next steps:                                   │
│ 1. Review auto-fixes: git diff                │
│ 2. Address manual updates if desired          │
│ 3. Commit documentation changes               │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

docs.title: 5-Phase Pipeline (v2.9.0)
docs.text:
The post-merge documentation update runs an automated pipeline:

Phase 1: Detect Stale Documentation

  • Analyzes files changed in last merge
  • Identifies related documentation files
  • Suggests specific updates needed
  • Uses git history to detect likely staleness

Phase 2: Validate Internal Links

  • Checks all markdown links in documentation
  • Detects broken links from file moves/deletions
  • Identifies redirect opportunities
  • Reports external link status (optional)

Phase 3: Auto-Fix Safe Issues

  • Updates redirected links automatically
  • Removes links to deleted files
  • Syncs version numbers (from package.json, pyproject.toml, etc.)
  • Updates navigation structures
  • Safe changes only (no content modification)

Phase 4: Generate Update Recommendations

  • Prioritizes manual updates (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
  • Provides specific guidance for each file
  • Estimates time for each update
  • Can open files for editing

Phase 5: Rebuild Documentation Site

  • Runs documentation build (mkdocs, Quarto, pkgdown)
  • Validates structure and links
  • Generates search index
  • Verifies no build errors

docs.title: Why Use --post-merge?
docs.text:
Without --post-merge:

  • /craft:docs:update does general documentation updates
  • Focuses on completeness, not recent changes

With --post-merge:

  • Analyzes specific merge commit
  • Targeted updates for changed files
  • Automated fixes for common post-merge issues
  • Faster, more focused workflow

docs.title: Variations
docs.text:
• Dry-run to preview changes:
/craft:docs:update --post-merge --dry-run

Shows what would be fixed without making changes

  • Auto-confirm all phases:
/craft:docs:update --post-merge --yes

Runs all phases without confirmation prompts

  • Skip specific phases:
/craft:docs:update --post-merge --skip-rebuild

Skips phase 5 (useful if site build is slow)

  • Target specific merge commit:
/craft:docs:update --post-merge --commit abc1234

Analyzes a specific merge instead of latest

  • Manual intervention for recommendations:
/craft:docs:update --post-merge --interactive

Opens each recommended file for editing

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "No merge commit detected" Ensure you just merged a branch; if not, specify --commit flag "Phase 2 reports false positives" Some links may be external or intentionally broken (tests); ignore or add to .linkcheck-ignore "Auto-fix changed wrong content" Review git diff, revert if needed, file issue for improvement "Phase 5 build fails" Check build output for errors, may be unrelated to merge "Too many manual recommendations" Prioritize HIGH only, defer MEDIUM/LOW for later "Want to skip confirmations" Use --yes flag for non-interactive mode

docs.title: After Feature Merge
docs.text:
# Merge feature to dev
gh pr merge 123 --squash

# Update documentation
/craft:docs:update --post-merge

# Review and commit
git diff                    # Review auto-fixes
git add -u                  # Stage documentation updates
git commit -m "docs: post-merge documentation update"
git push

docs.title: After Release Merge
docs.text:
# Merge dev to main
gh pr merge 456 --merge

# Comprehensive documentation update
/craft:docs:update --post-merge --mode thorough

# Deploy updated docs
/craft:site:deploy

docs.title: Example: Complete Post-Merge Workflow
docs.text:
# Scenario: Just merged feature/new-command to dev

# Step 1: Run post-merge pipeline
/craft:docs:update --post-merge

# Pipeline detects:
# - commands/new-command.md needs examples
# - REFCARD.md needs new command added
# - Version number needs sync

# Step 2: Review phase 1 output
# Shows 3 stale files detected
# Confirm: y

# Step 3: Phase 2 validates links
# Found 2 broken links (expected - new command not in nav yet)
# Confirm: y

# Step 4: Phase 3 auto-fixes
# ✓ Added new command to mkdocs.yml nav
# ✓ Updated version in CLAUDE.md
# ✓ Fixed broken links
# Confirm: y

# Step 5: Phase 4 recommendations
# HIGH: Add examples to commands/new-command.md
# MEDIUM: Add to REFCARD.md
# Choose: update (opens files)

# Step 6: Make manual edits
# Add examples, update refcard

# Step 7: Phase 5 rebuilds
# ✓ Site builds successfully

# Step 8: Commit changes
git diff                    # Review all changes
git add -u
git commit -m "docs: post-merge update for new-command feature"
git push

docs.title: Recipe: Quick Code Checks
docs.text: Time: 1 minute Level: Beginner Prerequisites: Craft installed, project with code files

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I want a fast code quality scan to catch obvious issues without waiting for a full test suite.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Run the linter in default mode
/craft:code:lint

Why: Default mode runs in under 10 seconds and catches formatting errors, style violations, and common mistakes

  1. Read the output
Linting results:
  ✓ Python: ruff (0 issues)
  ✗ Markdown: markdownlint (2 issues)
    docs/guide/setup.md:14  MD032  Blank line around list
    docs/guide/setup.md:28  MD040  Fenced code block without language

Why: Each issue includes the file, line number, rule ID, and a short description so you can fix it quickly

  1. Fix the reported issues

Edit the flagged files to resolve warnings. For markdown issues, the rule ID (e.g., MD032) tells you exactly what to change.

  1. Re-run to confirm
/craft:code:lint

Why: A clean run confirms all issues are resolved before you commit

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: /craft:code:lint detects your project type and runs the appropriate linters automatically. For Python projects it uses ruff or flake8; for Node.js it uses eslint; for documentation projects it runs markdownlint with 24 enforced rules. Default mode prioritizes speed over thoroughness. If you need deeper analysis, use debug mode for verbose traces or release mode for the full rule set.

docs.title: What's Next
docs.text:
• Code Commands Reference -- All code quality commands (format, coverage, ci-local)
  • Check Command Reference -- Full pre-flight validation before commits
  • Check Code Quality Before Commit -- Complete pre-commit workflow

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: You want to run only a subset of tests — fast ones during development, or domain-specific ones when working on a feature.

docs.title: By tier (speed)
docs.text:
/craft:test smoke           # < 30s total, quick validation
/craft:test unit             # Pure function tests, < 2s each
/craft:test integration      # Subprocess/filesystem, < 10s each
/craft:test e2e              # Full workflows, < 60s each

docs.title: By domain (feature area)
docs.text:
/craft:test hub              # Hub discovery tests
/craft:test branch_guard     # Branch protection
/craft:test claude_md        # CLAUDE.md sync/audit
/craft:test commands         # Command parsing
/craft:test release          # Release pipeline

docs.title: Combined filters
docs.text:
/craft:test "unit and hub"             # Unit tests for hub only
/craft:test "integration and not slow" # Fast integration tests
/craft:test "e2e and branch_guard"     # Branch guard e2e only

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Tests use pytest markers defined in pyproject.toml. Every test file has a module-level pytestmark:

pytestmark = [pytest.mark.unit, pytest.mark.hub]

The /craft:test command translates category arguments into -m flags for pytest.

docs.title: With debug output
docs.text: /craft:test unit debug       # Verbose output for unit tests

docs.title: With coverage
docs.text: /craft:test unit --coverage  # Unit tests with coverage report

docs.title: Specific file
docs.text: /craft:test --path tests/test_hub.py

docs.title: Name pattern filter
docs.text: /craft:test --filter "test_discover"   # Tests matching pattern

docs.title: Recipe: Set Up New Project
docs.text: Time: 5 minutes Level: Beginner Prerequisites: Git repository initialized

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I want to add Craft to my new project with recommended settings and git workflow patterns.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Navigate to your project
cd /path/to/your/project

Why: Craft needs to be run from your project root

  1. Initialize Craft
/craft:git:init

Why: Sets up recommended git workflow patterns, creates .claude/ directory, and configures project-specific settings

  1. Verify setup
/craft:check

Why: Runs pre-flight validation to ensure everything is configured correctly

  1. Review generated files
  2. .claude/settings.local.json — Project-specific Craft configuration
  3. .gitignore updates — Craft temporary files excluded
  4. Git hooks (optional) — Pre-commit validation

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
/craft:git:init performs the following:

  1. Creates .claude/ directory for project-specific configuration
  2. Detects project type (Node.js, Python, R package, Quarto, etc.)
  3. Sets up git patterns for the detected project type:
  4. Conventional commit templates
  5. Pre-commit hooks (if requested)
  6. Branch naming conventions
  7. Configures defaults based on project type (test runners, build commands, lint rules)
  8. Initializes worktree support for feature branch isolation

All settings are stored in .claude/settings.local.json and can be customized later.

docs.title: Variations
docs.text:

/craft:git:init --yes
  • Custom project type: Override auto-detection
/craft:git:init --type python
  • Skip git hooks: Initialize without pre-commit hooks
/craft:git:init --no-hooks

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "Not a git repository" Run git init first, then retry "Permission denied" Check write permissions in project directory Settings not applied Verify .claude/settings.local.json exists and is valid JSON Wrong project type detected Use --type flag to specify manually

docs.title: Recipe: Setup Parallel Worktrees
docs.text: Time: 8-10 minutes Level: Intermediate Prerequisites: Git repository, feature to implement NEW: v2.9.0 - Automatic ORCHESTRATE.md and SPEC.md generation

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I want to work on a feature branch in isolation without switching branches in my main repository. I also want automatic setup of orchestration files to plan my work.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Create worktree with automatic setup (v2.9.0)
/craft:git:worktree feature/user-authentication

What happens (v2.9.0 auto-setup):

╭─ Worktree Creation ──────────────────────────╮
│ Branch: feature/user-authentication           │
│ Location: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-...  │
│                                               │
│ Detected scope: feature implementation        │
│ Auto-generating:                              │
│ ✓ ORCHESTRATE.md (orchestration plan)         │
│ ✓ SPEC.md (technical specification)           │
│                                               │
│ Creating worktree...                          │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯
  1. Review generated files

The worktree automatically includes:

ORCHESTRATE.md - Orchestration plan template:

# ORCHESTRATE - User Authentication Feature

**Feature:** User authentication with JWT
**Branch:** feature/user-authentication
**Target:** dev
**Mode:** phase (recommended for features)

---

## Phase 1: Foundation - Auth Backend

### Files to Create/Modify
1. src/auth/jwt.ts (NEW) - JWT token handling
2. src/auth/middleware.ts (NEW) - Auth middleware
3. src/routes/auth.ts (NEW) - Auth routes

### Success Criteria
- [ ] JWT generation working
- [ ] Token validation working
- [ ] Tests passing

**Estimated:** 2-3 hours

---

## Phase 2: Integration - Connect to Existing System

[Auto-generated template continues...]

SPEC.md - Technical specification template:

# SPEC - User Authentication Feature

**Feature:** User authentication with JWT
**Branch:** feature/user-authentication
**Target Release:** v2.10.0

---

## Overview

Implement JWT-based authentication for the API.

[Auto-generated template continues with sections for:
 - User Stories
 - Architecture Decisions
 - API Changes
 - Database Schema
 - Testing Strategy
 - Security Considerations
 - Success Criteria]
  1. Navigate to worktree
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-user-authentication

Or use the provided alias:

# Shown in output after creation
alias wt-user-auth="cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-user-authentication"
  1. Review and customize the plan

Edit ORCHESTRATE.md to: - Adjust phase breakdown - Add specific file paths for your project - Refine success criteria - Update time estimates

Edit SPEC.md to: - Fill in architectural decisions - Add user stories - Define API contracts - Specify test requirements

  1. Start implementation

With your plan in place:

# Option 1: Use orchestrator with your plan
/craft:orchestrate --file ORCHESTRATE.md

# Option 2: Work phase by phase manually
# Refer to ORCHESTRATE.md as you work
# Check off tasks as you complete them
  1. When feature is complete
# In worktree directory
git push -u origin feature/user-authentication
gh pr create --base dev

# Then clean up worktree
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft  # Back to main repo
git worktree remove ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-user-authentication

docs.title: Automatic File Generation (v2.9.0)
docs.text:
The worktree command detects your branch pattern and generates appropriate files:

Branch patterns recognized:

Pattern Detected Scope Generated Files feature/* Feature implementation ORCHESTRATE.md (phase mode), SPEC.md fix/* Bug fix SPEC.md (focused on fix description) docs/* Documentation update ORCHESTRATE.md (docs phases) refactor/* Code refactoring ORCHESTRATE.md (refactor phases), SPEC.md experiment/* Experimental work SPEC.md (hypothesis, experiments)

docs.title: ORCHESTRATE.md Template
docs.text:
Auto-generated based on:

  • Branch name (extracts feature name)
  • Project type (detects craft plugin, R package, etc.)
  • Common phase patterns for that scope

For feature branches:

  • Phase 1: Foundation/Backend
  • Phase 2: Integration
  • Phase 3: Testing
  • Phase 4: Documentation

For docs branches:

  • Phase 1: Content update
  • Phase 2: Link validation
  • Phase 3: Build verification
  • Phase 4: Deployment

docs.title: SPEC.md Template
docs.text:
Includes sections:

  • Overview
  • User Stories (for features)
  • Architecture Decisions
  • API Changes
  • Testing Strategy
  • Success Criteria
  • Dependencies
  • Rollout Plan

docs.title: Variations
docs.text:
• Create worktree without auto-generation:
/craft:git:worktree feature/my-feature --no-setup

Creates worktree only, no ORCHESTRATE.md or SPEC.md

  • Create worktree with custom location:
/craft:git:worktree feature/my-feature --path ~/my-worktrees/my-feature

Uses custom directory instead of default ~/.git-worktrees

  • Create from specific base branch:
/craft:git:worktree feature/my-feature --from main

Branches from main instead of dev

  • Generate orchestration file in existing worktree:
cd ~/existing-worktree
/craft:git:worktree --generate-orch

Adds ORCHESTRATE.md to existing worktree

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "ORCHESTRATE.md not generated" Older version (<v2.9.0), update craft plugin "Generated files have wrong phase structure" Edit them manually, templates are starting points "Worktree location hard to remember" Use the provided alias shown in output "Want different base branch" Use --from flag: /craft:git:worktree feature/x --from main "ORCHESTRATE.md doesn't match my project" Templates are generic, customize for your needs "How to use orchestrator with ORCHESTRATE.md?" Run: /craft:orchestrate --file ORCHESTRATE.md

docs.title: Workflow 1: Parallel Feature Development
docs.text:
Work on 2 features simultaneously:

# Main repo stays on dev/main
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft

# Create first feature worktree
/craft:git:worktree feature/auth
# Location: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-auth

# Create second feature worktree
/craft:git:worktree feature/payments
# Location: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-payments

# Work on auth
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-auth
# Customize ORCHESTRATE.md, start work

# Switch to payments (different terminal)
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-payments
# Customize ORCHESTRATE.md, start work

# Both features in progress, no branch switching!

docs.title: Workflow 2: Experiment Without Disruption
docs.text:
Test a risky refactor:

/craft:git:worktree experiment/new-architecture

# Work in experiment worktree
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/experiment-new-architecture

# If experiment succeeds: merge to dev
git push -u origin experiment/new-architecture
gh pr create --base dev

# If experiment fails: just delete worktree
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft
git worktree remove ~/.git-worktrees/craft/experiment-new-architecture --force
git branch -D experiment/new-architecture
# Main repo unaffected!

docs.title: Workflow 3: Review PR in Isolated Worktree
docs.text:
Review someone's PR without switching your main branch:

# Create worktree from PR branch
/craft:git:worktree review/pr-123 --from origin/feature/their-feature

cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/review-pr-123
# Test their changes, run tests, review code

# When done, remove worktree
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft
git worktree remove ~/.git-worktrees/craft/review-pr-123

docs.title: Example: Complete Feature Workflow
docs.text:
# Step 1: Create worktree with auto-setup
/craft:git:worktree feature/oauth

# Output shows:
# ✓ ORCHESTRATE.md generated (4 phases)
# ✓ SPEC.md generated
# ✓ Worktree created at ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-oauth
# ✓ Alias: wt-oauth

# Step 2: Navigate and plan
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-oauth

# Step 3: Review and customize ORCHESTRATE.md
# Edit phase breakdown to match your project structure

# Step 4: Execute with orchestrator
/craft:orchestrate --file ORCHESTRATE.md --mode phase

# Step 5: Work through phases with checkpoints
# Orchestrator guides you through each phase
# Checkpoints after each phase for review

# Step 6: Complete and create PR
git push -u origin feature/oauth
gh pr create --base dev

# Step 7: Clean up after merge
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft
git worktree remove ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-oauth
git branch -d feature/oauth

docs.title: Recipe: Update a Single Documentation Section
docs.text: Time: 3 minutes Level: Beginner Prerequisites: Craft installed, MkDocs project with existing docs

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I want to update the documentation for one specific area of my project without regenerating everything.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Check which docs are outdated
/craft:docs:lint

Why: Scans all documentation files and reports stale content, broken links, and formatting issues so you know where to focus

  1. Update a specific documentation category
/craft:docs:update commands

Why: Targets only the commands docs directory, leaving other sections untouched. You can substitute any category name (e.g., guide, tutorials, reference)

  1. Review the proposed changes

The command shows a diff of what will change before applying:

Changes to apply:
  docs/commands/overview.md  - Updated command count (104 -> 106)
  docs/commands/code.md      - Added new flag documentation
Accept changes? (y/n)

Why: You confirm before anything is written, so you stay in control

  1. Verify the result
/craft:docs:lint

Why: Re-running the linter confirms the issues are resolved and no new problems were introduced

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: /craft:docs:update detects your project type and scans the specified section for outdated content. It checks command counts, version references, feature lists, and cross-references against the current codebase. Changes are always shown for review before being applied, so nothing is overwritten without your approval.

docs.text:
• Docs Commands Reference -- Full documentation command suite
  • Documentation Quality Guide -- Best practices for project docs
  • Check Code Quality Before Commit -- Validate everything before committing

docs.title: Recipe: Use Interactive Orchestration
docs.text: Time: 5-7 minutes Level: Intermediate Prerequisites: Complex task requiring multiple steps or agents NEW: v2.9.0 - Interactive mode selection and wave checkpoints

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I have a complex task that requires multiple steps, different specialized agents, or coordination across several files. I want to run it with visibility and control over the execution.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Start orchestration with task description
/craft:orchestrate "add user authentication with JWT, update docs, and add tests"

What happens (v2.9.0 "Show Steps First" pattern):

╭─ Task Analysis ──────────────────────────────╮
│ Task: add user authentication with JWT,      │
│       update docs, and add tests              │
│ Complexity: 8/10 (high)                       │
│ Estimated agents: 3                           │
│ Estimated time: 2-4 hours                     │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯
  1. Choose orchestration mode (v2.9.0)

You'll be asked:

Select orchestration mode:

1. default - Sequential execution (one step at a time)
   Best for: Simple multi-step tasks
   Time: Moderate

2. wave - Parallel waves with checkpoints
   Best for: Independent parallel tasks
   Time: Faster

3. phase - Sequential phases with validation
   Best for: Complex dependent tasks
   Time: Comprehensive

Which mode? (1/2/3)

Choose based on your task: - default (1): For straightforward tasks, no parallelization - wave (2): For tasks with independent steps that can run in parallel - phase (3): For complex tasks with dependencies and validation needs

  1. Review execution plan

After selecting mode, you'll see:

╭─ Execution Plan (wave mode) ─────────────────╮
│                                               │
│ Wave 1 (parallel):                            │
│   • backend-architect: JWT implementation     │
│   • security-specialist: Auth security review │
│                                               │
│ Wave 2 (after wave 1):                        │
│   • test-automator: Add auth tests            │
│   • docs-architect: Update auth documentation │
│                                               │
│ Checkpoints:                                  │
│   ✓ After wave 1: Review JWT implementation   │
│   ✓ After wave 2: Verify all tests pass       │
│                                               │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Proceed with this plan? (y/n/adjust)
  1. Confirm or adjust

Options: - y - Proceed with the plan - n - Cancel and revise your task description - adjust - Modify mode or agent selection

  1. Monitor execution with checkpoints (v2.9.0)

During execution:

╭─ Wave 1 Progress ────────────────────────────╮
│ [████████████░░░░░░░░] 60%                    │
│                                               │
│ ✓ backend-architect: JWT implementation done  │
│ ⟳ security-specialist: Reviewing...           │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

After each wave:

╭─ Wave 1 Checkpoint ──────────────────────────╮
│ Both agents completed successfully            │
│                                               │
│ Results:                                      │
│ • src/auth/jwt.ts created (145 lines)         │
│ • Security review: No critical issues found   │
│                                               │
│ Continue to wave 2? (y/n/adjust)              │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯
  1. Review final results

When complete:

╭─ Orchestration Complete ─────────────────────╮
│ All waves completed successfully              │
│                                               │
│ Summary:                                      │
│ • 4 agents executed                           │
│ • 8 files modified                            │
│ • 342 lines added                             │
│ • 89 lines removed                            │
│ • All tests passing (45 new tests)            │
│                                               │
│ Next steps:                                   │
│ 1. Review changes in modified files           │
│ 2. Run /craft:check --mode=thorough           │
│ 3. Commit changes                             │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

docs.title: Interactive Mode Selection (v2.9.0)
docs.text:
The orchestrator now asks you to choose how to execute:

default mode:

  • Sequential execution
  • One step completes before next begins
  • Best for: Simple multi-step tasks where order matters
  • Example: "Update docs then deploy"

wave mode:

  • Parallel waves with checkpoints
  • Independent tasks run simultaneously
  • Pauses between waves for review
  • Best for: Tasks with parallelizable steps
  • Example: "Update multiple documentation files" (parallel) → "Build site" (after all updates)

phase mode:

  • Sequential phases with validation
  • Each phase has clear deliverables
  • Comprehensive validation at checkpoints
  • Best for: Complex projects with dependencies
  • Example: "Phase 1: Design → Phase 2: Implement → Phase 3: Test → Phase 4: Document"

docs.title: Wave Checkpoints (v2.9.0)
docs.text:
After each wave completes:

  1. Shows results from all agents in that wave
  2. Asks for confirmation before continuing
  3. Allows adjustments if issues found
  4. Provides context for decision-making

Benefits:

  • Catch problems early before they cascade
  • Review intermediate results
  • Adjust plan based on actual outcomes
  • ADHD-friendly: natural break points in long tasks

docs.title: Variations
docs.text:
• Quick orchestration without mode selection:
/craft:orchestrate "simple task" --mode=default

Skips mode selection, goes directly to execution

  • Preview plan without executing:
/craft:orchestrate "task" --dry-run

Shows the execution plan but doesn't run it

  • Use with /craft:do for automatic mode selection:
/craft:do "complex multi-step task"

If complexity score is 8-10, automatically routes to orchestrator

  • Resume paused orchestration:
/craft:orchestrate:resume

Continue from last checkpoint if interrupted

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "Mode selection not showing" Update to v2.9.0+, older versions don't have interactive mode "Wave checkpoint hangs" One agent may be waiting for input, check agent output "Want to switch modes mid-execution" Cancel (Ctrl+C) and restart with different mode "Checkpoint shows errors" Review agent output, fix issues, then choose "adjust" to modify plan "Too many confirmation prompts" Use --mode flag to skip mode selection, or --yes to auto-confirm "Need to see what mode will do" Use --dry-run first to preview execution plan

docs.title: Use default mode when
docs.text:
• Task has 2-4 simple sequential steps
  • No parallelization possible
  • You want simplest execution
  • Example: "Lint code, run tests, commit"

docs.title: Use wave mode when
docs.text:
• Multiple independent subtasks
  • Can benefit from parallel execution
  • Want checkpoints to review progress
  • Example: "Update 5 documentation files + generate diagrams" (wave 1: parallel updates, wave 2: build site)

docs.title: Use phase mode when
docs.text:
• Complex multi-phase project
  • Strong dependencies between phases
  • Need comprehensive validation
  • Example: "Design API → Implement backend → Add tests → Update docs → Deploy" (must be sequential)

docs.title: Example 1: Documentation Update (wave mode)
docs.text:
/craft:orchestrate "update all tutorials for v2.9.0 features"

# Choose mode: 2 (wave)

# Execution:
# Wave 1 (parallel): Update 4 tutorial files
# Wave 2 (parallel): Update 3 reference cards
# Wave 3 (sequential): Rebuild docs site, validate links

# Result: 7 files updated in parallel, then validated

docs.title: Example 2: Feature Implementation (phase mode)
docs.text:
/craft:orchestrate "add OAuth2 authentication to API"

# Choose mode: 3 (phase)

# Execution:
# Phase 1: Design (security review, API design)
# Phase 2: Implement (backend code, database)
# Phase 3: Test (unit tests, integration tests)
# Phase 4: Document (API docs, tutorial)

# Each phase validated before next begins

docs.title: Example 3: Quick Multi-Step (default mode)
docs.text:
/craft:orchestrate "fix markdown lint errors and commit"

# Choose mode: 1 (default)

# Execution: Sequential
# 1. Run markdownlint --fix
# 2. Stage changes
# 3. Commit with message

# Simple, fast, no parallelization needed

docs.title: Recipe: View Project Architecture
docs.text: Time: 2 minutes Level: Beginner Prerequisites: Craft installed, existing project

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: I want a quick overview of my project's structure, dependencies, and key components without reading every file.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Run the architecture analyzer
/craft:arch:analyze

Why: Scans your project and produces a structured summary of directories, modules, dependencies, and entry points

  1. Review the output

The analyzer reports several sections:

Project: craft (Claude Code Plugin)
Type: Plugin (detected from .claude-plugin/)

Directory Structure:
  commands/    108 command files across 8 categories
  agents/      8 agent definitions
  skills/      21 skill modules
  utils/       7 Python utilities
  tests/       16 test files (1432 tests)

Key Entry Points:
  commands/do.md         Smart routing hub
  commands/hub.md        Command discovery
  commands/check.md      Pre-flight validation

Dependencies:
  Python 3.x, mkdocs, pytest, ruff

Why: Gives you a mental map of the project in seconds instead of manually exploring directories

  1. Explore a specific area
/craft:arch:analyze commands

Why: Narrows the analysis to one directory for a deeper look at that subsystem

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: /craft:arch:analyze detects your project type and walks the file tree to identify structure, key files, dependency relationships, and test coverage. It recognizes patterns for many project types: Node.js (package.json), Python (pyproject.toml), R packages (DESCRIPTION), Quarto sites (_quarto.yml), and Claude Code plugins (.claude-plugin/). The output adapts to what it finds, highlighting the most important architectural elements for that project type.

docs.title: What's Next
docs.text:

  • Craft Architecture Guide -- How the Craft plugin itself is built
  • Find the Right Command -- Discover more commands with the Hub

docs.title: Troubleshooting: Broken Links After Update
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
After updating documentation, mkdocs build or /craft:docs:check-links reports broken links:

WARNING - Doc file 'guide/my-guide.md' contains a link
  'tutorials/deleted-page.md', but the target is not found.

docs.title: 1. File Renamed Without Updating References
docs.text:
Issue: A file was renamed or moved, but other files still link to the old path.

Solution:

grep -r "old-name.md" docs/    # find all references
# Update each one to the new filename

Why: Markdown links use relative paths. When a file moves, every link to its old location breaks.

docs.title: 2. Deleted Page Still Referenced
docs.text:
Issue: A page was deleted but remains in mkdocs.yml nav or is linked from other pages.

Solution:

grep "deleted-page" mkdocs.yml   # check nav
grep -r "deleted-page.md" docs/  # check cross-references
# Remove or update each stale reference

Why: MkDocs validates that every nav entry and cross-reference resolves to an existing file.

docs.title: 3. Wrong Relative Path
docs.text:
Issue: The link uses an incorrect number of ../ levels.

Solution:

# From docs/guide/my-guide.md linking to docs/commands/do.md:
# Wrong:  [Do Command](commands/do.md)        -- missing ../
# Wrong:  [Do Command](../../commands/do.md)   -- too many levels
# Right:  [Do Command](../commands/do.md)      -- one level up

Why: Relative paths resolve from the directory of the file containing the link.

docs.title: 4. Expected Broken Links Not in Ignore List
docs.text:
Issue: Some links are intentionally broken (test fixtures, brainstorm drafts) but not in .linkcheck-ignore.

Solution:

echo "docs/brainstorm/draft-*.md" >> .linkcheck-ignore

docs.title: 5. Missing Nav Entry
docs.text:
Issue: A new page exists but is not in the nav section of mkdocs.yml.

Solution: Run mkdocs build 2>&1 | grep "not in the nav" and add the page to the correct nav section.

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
mkdocs build --strict 2>&1 | grep -i "warning"
/craft:docs:check-links
python3 tests/test_craft_plugin.py -k "broken_links"

A clean --strict build with zero warnings confirms all links are valid.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Documentation Commands -- All docs-related commands
  • Documentation Quality Guide -- Best practices for link management
  • Docs Update Reference -- Quick reference for docs updates

docs.title: Troubleshooting: CI Workflow Fails
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
A GitHub Actions workflow generated by Craft fails in CI:

Run npx markdownlint-cli2 "**/*.md"
Error: Cannot find module 'markdownlint-cli2'

docs.title: 1. Missing Dependencies in CI
docs.text:
Issue: The workflow references tools not installed in the CI runner.

Solution: Add installation steps before the command:

- name: Install dependencies
  run: npm install -g markdownlint-cli2
- name: Lint markdown
  run: npx markdownlint-cli2 "**/*.md"

Why: CI runners start clean. Local dependencies must be explicitly installed.

docs.title: 2. Wrong Node or Python Version
docs.text:
Issue: CI uses a different runtime version than local.

Solution: Pin versions in the workflow:

- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: '20'
- uses: actions/setup-python@v5
  with:
    python-version: '3.11'

docs.title: 3. Secrets Not Configured
docs.text:
Issue: Workflow references secrets not set in the repository.

Error: Input required and not supplied: token

Solution: Go to repo Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions and add the required secrets. Verify names match the workflow YAML.

docs.title: 4. YAML Syntax Errors
docs.text:
Issue: Invalid YAML that GitHub rejects.

Error: mapping values are not allowed in this context

Solution: Validate before pushing:

/craft:ci:validate

Common pitfalls: tabs instead of spaces, unquoted colons in values, missing block scalar indicators.

docs.title: 5. File Not Found or Not Executable
docs.text:
Issue: Workflow references missing files or files without execute permission.

Solution: Verify tracked (git ls-files) and make executable (git update-index --chmod=+x).

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
/craft:ci:validate                        # Validate workflow YAML
npx markdownlint-cli2 "**/*.md"          # Run checks locally
git push && gh run list --limit 1         # Push and check run

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• CI Generate Command — Generating CI workflows
  • Git Commands — Git workflow integration
  • Check Command — Pre-flight validation before push

docs.title: Troubleshooting: CLAUDE.md Out of Sync
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Your CLAUDE.md contains outdated information -- wrong command counts, missing features, or stale version numbers:

# Example of stale CLAUDE.md header
**94 commands** · **18 skills** · **6 agents**
**Current Version:** v2.8.0

When the project is actually at v2.18.0 with 108 commands, 25 skills, and 8 agents.

docs.title: 1. New Commands Added Without Syncing
docs.text:
Issue: Commands were added to commands/ but CLAUDE.md was not updated.

Solution:

/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

This runs the 4-phase pipeline: detect (scan project metrics) -> audit (compare against CLAUDE.md) -> fix (update stale values) -> optimize (enforce line budget and section priorities).

Why: CLAUDE.md references project metrics that change with every feature addition.

docs.title: 2. Version Bumped Without Sync
docs.text:
Issue: A release was tagged but CLAUDE.md still shows the old version.

Solution:

/craft:docs:claude-md:sync
grep "Current Version" CLAUDE.md   # verify fix

Why: The sync pipeline extracts the version from git tags and CHANGELOG.md, then updates the CLAUDE.md header.

docs.title: 3. Manual Edits That Drifted
docs.text:
Issue: Hand edits introduced inconsistencies -- duplicate sections, wrong counts, or broken links.

Solution:

/craft:docs:claude-md:sync
# Watch for optimizer warnings about bloated or duplicate sections

Why: The optimizer classifies sections by priority (P0/P1/P2) and flags sections that push the file over budget.

docs.title: 4. File Exceeds Line Budget
docs.text:
Issue: CLAUDE.md has grown beyond the recommended size, slowing down Claude's parsing.

Solution:

wc -l CLAUDE.md                          # check current size
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync               # optimizer trims low-priority content
bash scripts/claude-md-budget-check.sh   # pre-commit budget check

Why: CLAUDE.md is read at the start of every conversation. Lean files (< 150 lines for new projects) improve response quality and reduce token usage.

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
./scripts/validate-counts.sh             # verify command count matches reality
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync               # should report "0 issues found"
bash scripts/claude-md-budget-check.sh   # should pass without warnings

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• CLAUDE.md Command Reference -- init, sync, and edit commands
  • CLAUDE.md Quick Reference -- Cheat sheet for CLAUDE.md management

docs.title: Troubleshooting: Command Not Found
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
You're trying to run a Craft command but getting an error:

/craft:do check
-bash: /craft:do: No such file or directory

or

craft:check: command not found

docs.title: 1. Wrong Command Format
docs.text:
Issue: Using shell syntax instead of Claude Code slash command syntax

Wrong:

craft:check          # Shell format (won't work)
/craft:check         # Missing colon separator
craft check          # Missing prefix

Correct:

/craft:check         # Claude Code slash command format
/craft:do check      # Smart routing alternative

Why: Craft commands are Claude Code slash commands, not shell commands. They must start with /craft: when invoked in Claude Code.

docs.title: 2. Running in Wrong Environment
docs.text:
Issue: Trying to run Craft commands in a terminal instead of Claude Code

Solution: Craft commands only work inside Claude Code conversations:

  1. Open Claude Code application
  2. Navigate to your project directory
  3. Start a conversation
  4. Run /craft:check or other commands

Why: Craft is a Claude Code plugin that extends Claude's capabilities. It doesn't install shell commands.

docs.title: 3. Plugin Not Loaded
docs.text:
Issue: Craft plugin hasn't been loaded in the current session

Solution:

  1. Check if plugin is installed: Look for .claude-plugin/ directory in your Craft repository
  2. Restart Claude Code application
  3. Verify plugin location: ~/.claude/ or project-specific location
  4. Check plugin settings in ~/.claude/settings.json

Why: Claude Code loads plugins at startup from configured locations.

docs.title: 4. Typo in Command Name
docs.text:
Issue: Misspelling the command name

Common typos:

  • /craft:checks → /craft:check (extra 's')
  • /craft:do-check → /craft:do check (hyphen vs space)
  • /craft:git-init → /craft:git:init (hyphen vs colon)

Solution: Use tab completion or check Commands Reference for exact command names

docs.title: 5. Command Doesn't Exist
docs.text:
Issue: Trying to use a command that hasn't been created yet

Solution:

  1. Check the Commands Index for available commands
  2. Use /craft:do <task> for smart routing (it will suggest correct commands)
  3. Check if you need to update to a newer version of Craft

Example:

# Instead of guessing command names
/craft:validate-links  # Might not exist

# Use smart routing
/craft:do validate links  # Routes to correct command

docs.title: Check Plugin Installation
docs.text:
# In Claude Code conversation
/craft:help

If this works, the plugin is installed correctly.

docs.title: Check Available Commands
docs.text:
# In Claude Code conversation
/hub

This shows all available commands across all plugins.

docs.title: Validate Plugin Functionality
docs.text:
# In Claude Code conversation
/craft:check

This command validates your project setup and plugin installation. If this works, Craft is fully functional. See Check Command for details on what it validates.

docs.title: Reset Plugin Cache
docs.text:
Sometimes Claude Code's plugin cache gets stale:

  1. Exit Claude Code application
  2. Clear plugin cache (if you know where it's stored)
  3. Restart Claude Code
  4. Try command again

docs.title: Check Version Compatibility
docs.text:
Ensure you're using a compatible version:

  • Claude Code version: Check in Help → About
  • Craft version: Check in CHANGELOG.md or git tags
  • Required features: Some commands require specific Claude Code versions

docs.title: Ask for Help
docs.text:
If the issue persists:

  1. Check GitHub Issues
  2. Search for similar problems
  3. Create a new issue with:
  4. Exact command you tried
  5. Full error message
  6. Claude Code version
  7. Operating system

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Environment How to Run Craft Claude Code /craft:check (slash command) Terminal Not available (plugin only) Claude Desktop /craft:check (if plugin installed) claude.ai web Not available (requires Claude Code)

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Getting Started Guide — Complete setup instructions
  • ADHD Quick Start — 30-second quick start
  • Commands Index — Full command reference
  • Do Command — Smart routing for finding commands

docs.title: Troubleshooting: Command Times Out
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
A Craft command runs too long and exceeds its time budget:

/craft:code:lint release
⏳ Running comprehensive lint checks...
ERROR: Command exceeded 300s budget. Aborting.

docs.title: 1. Wrong Execution Mode for the Task
docs.text:
Issue: Using a heavy mode like release on a task that only needs the default.

Solution: Match the mode to the task:

Mode Budget Use When default < 10s Day-to-day tasks, quick checks debug < 120s Diagnosing unexpected behavior optimize < 180s Large codebases, deep scans release < 300s Pre-release validation only 
/craft:code:lint            # default — handles 90% of tasks
/craft:code:lint release    # overkill for a quick check

Why: The default mode handles most tasks. Reserve release for actual releases.

docs.title: 2. Complex Task in Default Mode
docs.text:
Issue: A genuinely complex task (large codebase, many files) times out at the 10-second default budget.

Solution: Upgrade the mode or use smart routing:

/craft:arch:analyze optimize         # More time for large codebases
/craft:do "analyze architecture"     # Auto-selects appropriate mode

Why: /craft:do uses the complexity scorer to automatically pick the right mode.

docs.title: 3. Network Issues for Site Commands
docs.text:
Issue: Site commands (site:update, link-check) stall on HTTP requests.

Solution:

  1. Check your network connection
  2. Use local-only checks if offline: /craft:docs:lint
  3. Retry with a longer budget: /craft:site:update optimize

Why: External link validation requires HTTP requests that can be slow on poor connections.

docs.title: 4. Stale Process or Cached State
docs.text:
Issue: A previous failed command left partial state that blocks the current run.

Solution:

  1. Cancel the stuck command (Ctrl+C)
  2. Run /craft:check to verify health
  3. Restart your Claude Code session if the problem persists

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
# Quick default-mode check (should complete in <10s)
/craft:check

# Verify mode selection works
/craft:code:lint debug
# Expected: completes within 120s with verbose output

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Commands Overview — Execution modes explained
  • Interactive Commands Guide — "Show Steps First" pattern
  • Do Command — Smart routing with automatic mode selection

docs.title: Troubleshooting: MkDocs Build Fails
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Running mkdocs build or mkdocs serve fails with an error:

ERROR - Config value 'theme': Uninstalled theme 'material'.
Aborted with 1 Configuration Errors!

or Unable to find 'guide/nonexistent-page.md' in the docs directory.

docs.title: 1. Missing Python Dependencies
docs.text:
Issue: The Material theme or required extensions are not installed.

Solution:

pip install mkdocs-material          # includes most extensions
pip install pymdown-extensions       # if pymdownx errors appear
mkdocs build                         # retry

Why: A fresh machine or new virtual environment will be missing these packages.

docs.title: 2. YAML Syntax Error in mkdocs.yml
docs.text:
Issue: Invalid YAML prevents MkDocs from parsing the config.

Solution:

python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('mkdocs.yml'))"

Common mistakes: tabs instead of spaces, missing colon after a key, wrong indentation in nav.

docs.title: 3. Nav Path Points to Missing File
docs.text:
Issue: A nav entry references a file that does not exist on disk.

Solution: Search mkdocs.yml for the filename from the error, then either create the missing file or correct the nav entry to match the actual path under docs/.

docs.title: 4. Duplicate Nav Entries
docs.text:
Issue: The same page appears twice in nav, producing a warning.

Solution:

grep -n "my-page.md" mkdocs.yml    # find duplicates
# Remove the extra entry

docs.title: 5. Extension Load Failure
docs.text:
Issue: A Markdown extension is misconfigured or its package is missing.

Solution:

pip install --upgrade pymdown-extensions

Why: Extension names must match their Python package exactly, and version mismatches cause load failures.

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
rm -rf site/                   # clean stale output
mkdocs build --strict          # strict mode treats warnings as errors
mkdocs serve                   # preview at http://127.0.0.1:8000

A successful --strict build with zero errors confirms the site is healthy.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Site Commands -- Commands for building and deploying the docs site
  • Documentation Quality Guide -- Best practices for maintaining docs

docs.title: Troubleshooting: Pre-Commit Blocks Commit
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Running git commit triggers pre-commit hooks and the commit is rejected:

$ git commit -m "feat: add new command"
markdownlint-cli2..................................................Failed
- hook id: markdownlint-cli2
docs/guide/new-feature.md:15 MD032/blanks-around-lists
docs/guide/new-feature.md:28 MD040/fenced-code-language

emoji-attr-spacing.................................................Failed
- hook id: emoji-attr-spacing
docs/index.md:42 CRAFT-001: space between emoji and attr_list

docs.title: 1. Markdownlint Failures (24 Rules)
docs.text:
Issue: Markdown files violate enforced rules. Common violations:

  • MD032 — Missing blank line before/after list
  • MD040 — Fenced code block without language tag
  • MD009 — Trailing whitespace

Solution:

npx markdownlint-cli2 "**/*.md"          # See all issues
npx markdownlint-cli2 --fix "**/*.md"    # Auto-fix

docs.title: 2. Emoji-Attribute Spacing (CRAFT-001)
docs.text:
Issue: Space between emoji shortcode and attr_list breaks MkDocs rendering.

:material-code-tags: { .lg .middle }   <!-- Wrong: space before { -->
:material-code-tags:{ .lg .middle }    <!-- Correct: no space -->

Solution:

bash scripts/docs-lint-emoji.sh          # Check violations
bash scripts/docs-lint-emoji.sh --fix    # Auto-fix

docs.title: 3. CLAUDE.md Budget Check Failure
docs.text:
Issue: CLAUDE.md exceeds the line budget (150-line target for new projects).

Solution:

  1. Run the optimizer: /craft:docs:claude-md:sync
  2. Move detail to docs/VERSION-HISTORY.md or docs/ARCHITECTURE.md

docs.title: 4. Bypassing Hooks (Emergency Only)
docs.text:
git commit --no-verify -m "wip: urgent fix, lint cleanup follows"

Use sparingly. CI will still catch issues on push.

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
npx markdownlint-cli2 "**/*.md"           # Check markdown
bash scripts/docs-lint-emoji.sh            # Check CRAFT-001
bash scripts/claude-md-budget-check.sh     # Check budget
git add -A && git commit -m "feat: add new command"  # Retry

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Documentation Quality Guide — Full lint rule reference
  • Code Commands — Lint and quality commands

docs.title: Troubleshooting: Worktree Won't Create
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Running /craft:git:worktree feature/<name> fails instead of creating a new worktree:

fatal: 'feature/docs-revamp' already exists

or

fatal: '/Users/you/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-docs-revamp' already exists

docs.title: 1. Branch Already Exists
docs.text:
Issue: A local branch with the same name exists from a previous worktree.

Solution:

git branch --list 'feature/*'
git branch -d feature/docs-revamp   # safe if already merged
/craft:git:worktree feature/docs-revamp

Why: Git refuses to create a worktree for a branch that already exists locally.

docs.title: 2. Directory Path Conflict
docs.text:
Issue: The target directory exists on disk even though no worktree is registered.

Solution:

rm -rf ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-docs-revamp
git worktree prune
/craft:git:worktree feature/docs-revamp

Why: git worktree prune cleans internal references but does not delete orphaned directories.

docs.title: 3. Not on the dev Branch
docs.text:
Issue: Worktrees must be created from dev to branch from the correct base.

Solution:

git branch --show-current        # check current branch
git checkout dev && git pull origin dev
/craft:git:worktree feature/docs-revamp

Why: Craft requires main <- dev <- feature/*. Branching from main or another feature branch breaks the integration flow.

docs.title: 4. Uncommitted Changes
docs.text:
Issue: A dirty working tree blocks the branch operation.

Solution:

git stash push -m "stash before worktree"
/craft:git:worktree feature/docs-revamp

Why: Worktree creation involves checkout operations that conflict with uncommitted changes.

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
git worktree list
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-docs-revamp
git branch --show-current
# Expected: feature/docs-revamp

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Worktree Command Reference -- Full command documentation
  • Worktree Advanced Patterns -- Multi-worktree management
  • Git Worktree Quick Reference -- Cheat sheet

docs.title: Troubleshooting: Wrong Command Output
docs.text: Level: Beginner

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
A Craft command runs without errors but produces unexpected output:

/craft:code:lint debug
🔍 Debug mode: verbose trace enabled
[... 200 lines of trace output ...]
✅ Lint complete (0 issues)

You expected a simple summary but got a wall of debug information.

docs.title: 1. Mode Mismatch
docs.text:
Issue: debug mode produces verbose traces; default mode suppresses detail.

/craft:code:lint            # Concise: shows only issues found
/craft:code:lint debug      # Verbose: every file, every rule
/craft:code:lint release    # Thorough: full analysis with performance data

Why: Each mode adjusts both analysis depth and output verbosity.

docs.title: 2. Wrong Command for the Task
docs.text:
Issue: You used a specific command when a different one was appropriate.

Solution: Use /craft:do for smart routing:

/craft:do "check code quality"        # Routes to code:lint
/craft:do "analyze project structure"  # Routes to arch:analyze
/craft:do "validate before commit"     # Routes to check

docs.title: 3. Outdated Cached Data
docs.text:
Issue: Output references stale counts or old metrics.

Solution:

./scripts/validate-counts.sh       # Refresh cached metrics
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync         # Sync CLAUDE.md metrics

docs.title: 4. Wrong Flags or Arguments
docs.text:
Issue: Flags change behavior unexpectedly.

/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --fix    # Applies auto-fixes
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync          # Audit-only, shows what would change

Solution: Use the "Show Steps First" pattern -- commands preview their plan before executing.

docs.title: 5. Project Context Not Detected
docs.text:
Issue: Output seems generic because project type was not auto-detected.

Solution: Ensure your project has a recognized marker: DESCRIPTION (R), pyproject.toml (Python), package.json (Node), _quarto.yml (Quarto), or .claude-plugin/ (Craft plugin).

docs.title: Verification Steps
docs.text:
/craft:do "lint my code"         # Verify smart routing
./scripts/validate-counts.sh     # Validate metrics
/craft:check                     # Quick sanity check

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Do Command — Smart routing with complexity scoring
  • Commands Overview — All execution modes and flags

docs.title: Interactive Documentation Update - Example Walkthrough
docs.text: This example demonstrates the /craft:docs:update --interactive workflow from detection through completion.

docs.title: Initial State
docs.text:
Project: craft v2.5.1 Recent changes:

  • Added 2 new commands
  • Updated orchestrator mode selection
  • Fixed 3 broken links

docs.title: Step 1: User Invocation
docs.text: bash /craft:docs:update --interactivetext

docs.title: Step 2: Detection Phase
docs.text:
Claude runs both utilities:

```python from utils.docs_detector import DocsDetector from utils.help_file_validator import HelpFileValidator

detector = DocsDetector('.') validator = HelpFileValidator('.')

detection_results = detector.detect_all("v2.5.1") validation_issues = validator.validate_all() ```text

Detection Results:

text Version References: 12 items need updating Command Counts: 4 items need updating Broken Links: 3 items need fixing Missing Help: 5 commands need help Outdated Status: 2 items need updatingtext

docs.title: Prompt 1: Metadata Updates
docs.text:
Claude uses AskUserQuestion:

text ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮ │ Documentation Updates Available │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Question 1/4: Version References (12 items) │ │ │ │ Should I update version references? │ │ Option A: Yes, update all (12 files → v2.5.1) │ │ Option B: Preview changes first │ │ Option C: Skip this category │ │ │ │ Question 2/4: Command Counts (4 items) │ │ │ │ Should I update command counts? │ │ Option A: Yes, update all (99 → 101 commands) │ │ Option B: Preview changes first │ │ Option C: Skip this category │ │ │ ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯text

User Response: A, A (update both)

docs.title: Prompt 2: Link Issues
docs.text:
text ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮ │ Link Validation Issues │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Question 1/2: Broken Links (3 items) │ │ │ │ Should I fix these broken links? │ │ • docs/guide/old-feature.md → guide/features.md │ │ • commands/legacy.md (file doesn't exist) │ │ • README.md → #missing-anchor │ │ │ │ Option A: Yes, fix all (3 broken links) │ │ Option B: Fix one-by-one │ │ Option C: Skip this category │ │ │ ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯text

User Response: A (fix all)

docs.title: Prompt 3: Documentation Completeness
docs.text:
text ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮ │ Documentation Completeness │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Question 1/2: Missing Help (5 commands) │ │ │ │ Should I add help documentation? │ │ Commands without help: │ │ • /craft:test:gen │ │ • /craft:test:template │ │ • /craft:git:worktree │ │ • /craft:hub │ │ • /craft:discovery-usage │ │ │ │ Option A: Yes, add all (5 commands) │ │ Option B: Select specific commands │ │ Option C: Skip this category │ │ │ │ Question 2/2: Outdated Status (2 items) │ │ │ │ Should I update status markers? │ │ • Feature X: WIP → Complete │ │ • Feature Y: Draft → Active │ │ │ │ Option A: Yes, update all │ │ Option B: Skip this category │ │ │ ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯text

User Response: C, A (skip help, update status)

docs.title: Step 4: Applying Updates
docs.text:
Claude applies selected updates:

text ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮ │ Applying Updates... │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ ✓ Version References: 12 files updated │ │ README.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/guide/installation.md, ... │ │ │ │ ✓ Command Counts: 4 files updated │ │ README.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/architecture.md, ... │ │ │ │ ✓ Broken Links: 3 fixed │ │ docs/guide/old-feature.md → guide/features.md │ │ commands/legacy.md → commands/current.md │ │ README.md anchor fixed │ │ │ │ ⊘ Missing Help: Skipped by user │ │ │ │ ✓ Outdated Status: 2 updated │ │ Feature X: WIP → Complete │ │ Feature Y: Draft → Active │ │ │ ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯text

docs.title: Step 5: Summary Report
docs.text: text ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮ │ ✅ DOCUMENTATION UPDATE COMPLETE │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Interactive Mode Summary: │ │ │ │ Categories Processed: │ │ ✓ Version references: 12 files updated │ │ ✓ Command counts: 4 files updated │ │ ✓ Broken links: 3 fixed │ │ ⊘ Missing help: Skipped by user │ │ ✓ Outdated status: 2 updated │ │ │ │ Files Modified: 21 │ │ Total Changes: 41 updates │ │ Time: 2.3 seconds │ │ │ │ ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── │ │ │ │ NEXT STEPS: │ │ │ │ 1. Review changes: │ │ git diff │ │ │ │ 2. Run tests: │ │ /craft:test │ │ │ │ 3. Commit: │ │ git add . │ │ git commit -m "docs: update version refs, counts, links" │ │ │ │ 4. Remaining tasks: │ │ • 5 commands still need help documentation │ │ │ ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯text

docs.title: Scenario A: Category-Specific Update
docs.text:
bash /craft:docs:update --interactive --category=broken_linkstext

Only prompts for broken links category, skips others.

docs.title: Scenario B: Auto-Yes Mode
docs.text:
bash /craft:docs:update --auto-yestext

Applies all updates without prompts (use with caution).

docs.title: Scenario C: Dry-Run Preview
docs.text:
bash /craft:docs:update --interactive --dry-runtext

Shows what would be updated without applying changes.

docs.title: Implementation Notes
docs.text:
1. Grouping Logic: Related categories are grouped into single prompts (max 4 questions each)
  2. Preview Support: Users can request detailed preview before applying
  3. Selective Updates: Users can choose specific items within categories
  4. Error Handling: Failed updates are reported but don't block other categories
  5. Rollback: Changes are applied atomically per category (can revert if needed)

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Validate the workflow:

```bash

docs.title: Test utilities
docs.text: python3 tests/test_docs_utilities.py

docs.title: Test interactive command (dry-run)
docs.text: /craft:docs:update --interactive --dry-run

docs.title: Test specific category
docs.text: /craft:docs:update --interactive --category=version_refs

docs.title: Test auto-yes
docs.text:
/craft:docs:update --auto-yes --dry-run ```text

All 7 integration tests should pass before deployment.

docs.title: Badge Management Guide
docs.text: Automatic badge synchronization for README.md and docs/index.md

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The badge management system ensures version, CI status, and documentation coverage badges stay in sync across README.md and docs/index.md. Badge sync integrates into existing commands (/craft:site:update, /craft:docs:update) and provides CI-specific validation.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Auto-sync during site updates - Step 3.5 in /craft:site:update
  • Version badge generation - Extracts from plugin.json, package.json, pyproject.toml
  • CI badge generation - Scans .github/workflows/*.yml files
  • Coverage badges - Calculates from .STATUS file
  • Branch-aware validation - Checks badge branch parameters match current branch
  • Multi-file sync - Updates README.md and docs/index.md together
  • 4 project types - Craft plugins, Python CLI, Node CLI, teaching sites

docs.title: Badge Types
docs.text: Type Generated From Example Version plugin.json, package.json, pyproject.toml [![Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/version-2.10.0--dev-blue.svg)](...) CI Status .github/workflows/*.yml files [![CI](https://github.com/user/repo/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg?branch=dev)](...) Docs Coverage .STATUS file "Documentation: XX%" [![Documentation](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-98%25%20complete-brightgreen.svg)](...) Test Coverage External service (codecov, coveralls) [![Coverage](https://codecov.io/gh/user/repo/badge.svg)](...) Custom User-defined badges Any other badge format

docs.title: 1. Site Update (Primary Integration)
docs.text:
Badge sync runs automatically during /craft:site:update as Step 3.5:

/craft:site:update

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
Step 1: Detect Changes
Step 2: Update Detection Matrix
Step 3: Execute Updates
  ├─ Update REFCARD.md
  ├─ Update commands.md
  ├─ Update index.md
  └─ Update Configuration Reference

Step 3.5: Synchronize Badges  ← NEW
  ├─ Detect badge mismatches
  ├─ Show diff (version, CI, coverage)
  ├─ Prompt for approval
  └─ Apply updates

Step 4: Validate
Step 5: Show Results

docs.title: 2. Docs Update (Detection Integration)
docs.text:
Badge issues are detected as a metadata category alongside version_refs and command_counts:

/craft:docs:update

Documentation Update Plan:
  Categories detected:
  1. Badges — 3 badges outdated (version, branch)
  2. Version references — 12 files need updating
  3. Command counts — 4 files with outdated counts

  Group 1: Metadata updates
    • badges (3 issues)
    • version_refs (12 issues)
    • command_counts (4 issues)

  How should I handle these updates?
    → Interactive - category by category (Recommended)

docs.title: 3. CI Validate (Badge Checks)
docs.text:
CI badge validation checks workflow existence and branch parameters:

/craft:ci:validate

docs.title: Validation Checks
docs.text: Check Description Severity Workflow Exists Badge points to actual .github/workflows/*.yml file Error Branch Correct Badge ?branch= parameter matches current branch Warning URL Format Badge follows GitHub Actions URL pattern Warning

docs.title: Output Example
docs.text:
╭─ CI Badge Validation ───────────────────────────╮
│                                                  │
│  📁 README.md                                    │
│                                                  │
│    ❌ Line 3: missing_workflow                  │
│       Badge points to non-existent workflow     │
│                                                  │
│    ⚠️  Line 4: wrong_branch                     │
│       Badge uses 'main', expected 'dev'         │
│                                                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Total: 1 error, 1 warning                      │
│  Run with --fix to update badges                │

After creating a workflow, offer to add CI badge:

/craft:ci:generate python
