    Change base branch (currently: dev)
    Change location
    Cancel

Choose "Yes - Create worktree".

Execution:

[1/4] Creating directory... ✅
  Created: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth

[2/4] Creating branch from dev... ✅
  Branch: feature/add-auth
  Base: dev
  Commits ahead: 0

[3/4] Installing dependencies... ✅
  Detected: Python (pyproject.toml)
  Ran: pip install -e .
  Installed: 23 packages

[4/4] Auto-setup workflow files...

docs.title: Step 4.1: Scope Detection Prompt
docs.text:
After creating the worktree, craft detects the scope from your branch pattern:

Scope Detection:
  Branch: feature/add-auth
  Pattern: feature/* → Medium scope

Medium scope features typically:
  - Add new functionality (not just bug fixes)
  - Require planning across multiple files
  - Take 2-8 hours of focused work
  - Need orchestration or spec documents

? What workflow files should I create?
  > Create ORCHESTRATE file (Recommended)
    Multi-phase project (ORCHESTRATE + SPEC + .STATUS + CLAUDE.md)
    Minimal (no files)
    Custom

Scope Detection Rules:

Branch Pattern Detected Scope Auto-Creates fix/* Small (< 2 hours) Nothing feature/* Medium (2-8 hours) ORCHESTRATE file v* (e.g., v2.9.0) Release ORCHESTRATE + SPEC User chooses "Multi-phase" Large (> 8 hours) All files

docs.title: Step 4.2: ORCHESTRATE File Creation
docs.text:
Choose "Create ORCHESTRATE file":

Creating workflow files...

  ✅ ORCHESTRATE-add-auth.md (template created)

Template includes:
  - Feature overview
  - Acceptance criteria
  - Implementation phases
  - How to start
  - Testing checklist

Worktree ready!
  Location: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth
  Branch: feature/add-auth
  Files: ORCHESTRATE-add-auth.md

Next steps:
  1. cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth
  2. Review and customize ORCHESTRATE-add-auth.md
  3. Start development

docs.title: Step 4.3: What the ORCHESTRATE Template Contains
docs.text:
# Feature: Add Auth

**Status:** WIP
**Branch:** feature/add-auth
**Target:** Implement OAuth 2.0 authentication

## Overview

[Describe the feature and its purpose]

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Users can sign in with OAuth 2.0
- [ ] Sessions persist across browser restarts
- [ ] Logout functionality works correctly
- [ ] Tests cover happy path and edge cases

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Backend Routes (2 hours)
- [ ] Create `/auth/login` endpoint
- [ ] Create `/auth/logout` endpoint
- [ ] JWT token generation and validation

### Phase 2: Frontend Integration (3 hours)
- [ ] Login page UI
- [ ] Session management
- [ ] Protected route guards

### Phase 3: Testing (2 hours)
- [ ] Unit tests for auth endpoints
- [ ] Integration tests for OAuth flow
- [ ] E2E tests for user login/logout

## How to Start

1. Review this plan and adjust phases
2. Start with Phase 1
3. Check off items as you complete them
4. Run `/craft:check` before committing
5. Create PR when all acceptance criteria met

## Notes

[Add design decisions, dependencies, or blockers here]

Why this is useful:

  • Gives you a structured starting point
  • Breaks work into phases
  • Tracks acceptance criteria
  • Serves as PR description draft

docs.title: Step 5.1: Navigate to the Worktree
docs.text:
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth

Verify you're on the right branch:

git branch --show-current
# Should show: feature/add-auth

docs.title: Step 5.2: Customize the ORCHESTRATE File
docs.text:
# Open in your editor
code ORCHESTRATE-add-auth.md  # VS Code
vim ORCHESTRATE-add-auth.md   # Vim

Fill in the details based on your feature requirements.

docs.title: Step 5.3: Start Development
docs.text:
# Start Claude Code session
claude

# Or work manually
git status
# Make changes to files
git add .
git commit -m "feat: add OAuth login endpoint"

docs.title: Step 5.4: Track Progress
docs.text:
As you work, check off items in ORCHESTRATE-add-auth.md:

### Phase 1: Backend Routes
- [x] Create `/auth/login` endpoint
- [x] Create `/auth/logout` endpoint
- [ ] JWT token generation and validation

docs.title: Step 6.1: When to Use Multi-Phase Setup
docs.text:
For larger features (> 8 hours), choose "Multi-phase project" during auto-setup:

/craft:git:worktree create feature/v2.9.0

Scope detection:

Scope Detection:
  Branch: feature/v2.9.0
  Pattern: v* → Release scope

? What workflow files should I create?
    Create ORCHESTRATE file
  > Multi-phase project (ORCHESTRATE + SPEC + .STATUS + CLAUDE.md)
    Minimal (no files)
    Custom

Choose "Multi-phase project".

docs.title: Step 6.2: Files Created
docs.text:
Creating workflow files...

  ✅ ORCHESTRATE-v2.9.0.md
  ✅ docs/specs/SPEC-v2.9.0-2026-01-29.md
  ✅ .STATUS (updated with worktree info)
  ✅ CLAUDE.md (updated with active worktrees)

Files created:
  - ORCHESTRATE-v2.9.0.md — Task plan with phases
  - docs/specs/SPEC-v2.9.0-2026-01-29.md — Requirements and design
  - .STATUS — Marks branch as WIP in project status
  - CLAUDE.md — Adds worktree to "Active Development" section

SPEC file template:

# SPEC: v2.9.0 Release

**Created:** 2026-01-29
**Status:** Draft
**Branch:** feature/v2.9.0

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements
- FR1: [Requirement 1]
- FR2: [Requirement 2]

### Non-Functional Requirements
- NFR1: Performance targets
- NFR2: Security requirements

## Design Decisions

### Decision 1: [Topic]
**Options considered:** A, B, C
**Chosen:** B
**Rationale:** [Why B is best]

## Implementation Plan

See ORCHESTRATE-v2.9.0.md for detailed task breakdown.

## Testing Strategy

- Unit tests: [Scope]
- Integration tests: [Scope]
- E2E tests: [Scenarios]

docs.title: Step 7.1: Pre-Finish Checklist
docs.text:
Before running /craft:git:worktree finish, verify:

# 1. All changes committed
git status
# Should show: nothing to commit, working tree clean

# 2. Branch up to date with dev
git fetch origin dev
git rebase origin/dev

# 3. Tests pass
/craft:check

docs.title: Step 7.2: Run Finish Workflow
docs.text:
/craft:git:worktree finish

Output:

Finish Feature Workflow:
  Branch: feature/add-auth
  Commits: 7 since branching from dev
  Target: dev

  Steps:
  1. Run tests
  2. Generate changelog entry
  3. Remove ORCHESTRATE files (merge cleanup)
  4. Create pull request

? Proceed with finish workflow?
  > Yes - Complete feature (Recommended)
    Skip tests (faster)
    Draft PR (don't notify reviewers)
    Cancel

[1/4] Running tests... ✅
  Detected: Python (pytest)
  Ran: pytest -v
  Result: 47/47 passed

[2/4] Generating changelog... ✅
  Branch type: feature/* → "Added" section
  Entry: "Add OAuth 2.0 authentication system"

[3/4] Removing ORCHESTRATE files... ✅
  Found: ORCHESTRATE-add-auth.md
  Removed (merge cleanup)

[4/4] Creating pull request... ✅
  Title: feat: Add OAuth 2.0 authentication
  Base: dev
  PR: https://github.com/Data-Wise/craft/pull/42

Finish workflow complete!

Next steps:
  1. Review PR: gh pr view 42
  2. Request reviews
  3. After merge: /craft:git:worktree clean

docs.title: Step 7.3: Clean Up After Merge
docs.text:
After the PR is merged:

# Go back to main repo
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft

# Pull the merged changes
git checkout dev
git pull origin dev

# Clean up the merged worktree
/craft:git:worktree clean

Output:

Cleaning merged worktrees...

Checking: feature-add-auth
  Branch: feature/add-auth
  Merged: ✓ (merged into dev)
  Location: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth

? Remove worktree for merged branch feature/add-auth?
  > Yes - Remove (Recommended)
    Keep for reference
    Skip

[1/2] Removing worktree... ✅
  Removed: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth

[2/2] Deleting local branch... ✅
  Deleted: feature/add-auth

Cleanup complete!
  Removed: 1 worktree
  Remaining: 0 active worktrees

docs.title: Scenario 1: Urgent Hotfix While Working on Feature
docs.text:
# You're working in feature worktree
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth

# Urgent bug comes in
# Don't stash! Just create a new worktree:
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft
/craft:git:worktree create fix/urgent-login-bug

# Work on hotfix in separate folder
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/fix-urgent-login-bug
# Fix the bug, commit, create PR

# Go back to feature work
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth
# Your work is exactly as you left it!

docs.title: Scenario 2: You Forgot to Create a Worktree
docs.text:
# You're on feature/add-auth in the main repo with uncommitted work
cd ~/projects/dev-tools/craft
git branch --show-current
# Oops: feature/add-auth (should be on main!)

# Use the "move" action to fix this
/craft:git:worktree move

Output:

Move Branch to Worktree:
  Current branch: feature/add-auth
  Uncommitted files: 15

  This will:
  1. Stash your work
  2. Switch main repo to 'main'
  3. Create worktree for feature/add-auth
  4. Restore your stashed work in the worktree

? Move feature/add-auth to worktree?
  > Yes - Move (Recommended)
    Cancel

[1/5] Stashing work... ✅
  Stashed: 15 files

[2/5] Switching to main... ✅
  Main repo now on: main

[3/5] Creating worktree... ✅
  Location: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth

[4/5] Restoring work... ✅
  Applied stash: 15 files restored

[5/5] Installing dependencies... ✅

Done! Your setup:
  Main repo: ~/projects/dev-tools/craft (main)
  Feature work: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth

All 15 uncommitted files are now in the worktree.

docs.title: Scenario 3: Running Dev Servers in Parallel
docs.text:
# Create worktrees for frontend and backend work
/craft:git:worktree create feature/frontend-redesign
/craft:git:worktree create feature/api-v2

# Terminal 1: Run frontend dev server
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-frontend-redesign
npm run dev -- --port 3000

# Terminal 2: Run backend dev server
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-api-v2
npm run dev -- --port 4000

# Terminal 3: Code changes
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-frontend-redesign
claude  # Work on frontend while both servers run

docs.title: Issue: "Branch already exists"
docs.text:
# Error
git: branch 'feature/add-auth' already exists

# Solution: Use existing branch
/craft:git:worktree create feature/add-auth --use-existing

# Or: Delete and recreate
git branch -D feature/add-auth
/craft:git:worktree create feature/add-auth

docs.title: Issue: Dependencies Not Installed
docs.text:
# After creating worktree, dependencies are missing

# Solution: Install manually
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth
/craft:git:worktree install

# Or run project-specific install
npm install        # Node.js
pip install -e .   # Python

docs.title: Issue: Can't Delete Worktree
docs.text:
# Error
fatal: worktree still has modifications

# Solution 1: Force remove
git worktree remove ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth --force

# Solution 2: Commit or discard changes first
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-add-auth
git status
git add . && git commit -m "WIP"
# Then remove

docs.title: Issue: Worktree Path Too Long (macOS)
docs.text:
# Path is too long for macOS
~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-very-long-descriptive-branch-name/

# Solution: Use shorter location
/craft:git:worktree create feature/short-name

docs.title: After Brainstorm: Creating ORCHESTRATE Files
docs.text: If you used /brainstorm to plan a feature and captured a spec, you can create an ORCHESTRATE file and worktree in one step:

docs.title: From Brainstorm to Worktree
docs.text:
# 1. Brainstorm produces a spec
/brainstorm d:8 f "user notifications"
# → BRAINSTORM-notifications-2026-02-15.md saved
# → "Capture as spec?" → Yes
# → docs/specs/SPEC-notifications-2026-02-15.md saved

# 2. Create ORCHESTRATE + worktree from the spec
/craft:orchestrate:plan docs/specs/SPEC-notifications-2026-02-15.md

# Output:
# Orchestration Plan:
#   Spec: SPEC-notifications-2026-02-15.md
#   Phases found: 3 (setup, implementation, integration)
#   Branch: feature/notifications
#   Worktree: ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-notifications
#
# ? Proceed?
#   > Yes - Create ORCHESTRATE + worktree (Recommended)
#     ORCHESTRATE only (no worktree)
#     Modify phases
#     Cancel
#
# Created:
#   ✅ ORCHESTRATE-notifications.md (in worktree root)
#   ✅ Worktree at ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-notifications
#   ✅ .STATUS updated with worktree entry

# 3. Start working
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-notifications
claude
# → "Read ORCHESTRATE-notifications.md and start Phase 1"

docs.title: What the ORCHESTRATE File Contains
docs.text:
The generated ORCHESTRATE file includes:

  • Objective — pulled from spec summary
  • Phase breakdown — with tasks, priorities, and effort estimates
  • Friction prevention — based on insights from past sessions
  • Acceptance criteria — from the spec
  • Verification commands — test and validation commands for the project
  • Session instructions — how to start, what to read first, phase-by-phase guidance

docs.title: Without a Spec
docs.text:
If you don't have a spec, /craft:orchestrate:plan will scan for available specs and brainstorms:

/craft:orchestrate:plan

# Output:
# Available specs:
#   1. SPEC-auth-2026-02-10.md (no ORCHESTRATE yet)
#   2. SPEC-dashboard-2026-02-12.md (has ORCHESTRATE)
#
# Brainstorms without specs:
#   3. BRAINSTORM-caching-2026-02-14.md (offer to create spec first)
#
# ? Which one?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
You've learned:

  • What worktrees are and when to use them (parallel development without branch switching)
  • How to set up worktrees for your project (/craft:git:worktree setup)
  • How to create a worktree with auto-setup (/craft:git:worktree create)
  • What scope detection does and what files it creates (ORCHESTRATE, SPEC, .STATUS, CLAUDE.md)
  • How to work in a worktree (customize ORCHESTRATE, track progress)
  • How to finish and clean up (/craft:git:worktree finish, /craft:git:worktree clean)
  • Real-world scenarios (hotfixes, forgot to create worktree, parallel dev servers)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Command reference: /craft:git:worktree — Full documentation
  • Quick reference: Git Worktree Refcard — Cheat sheet
  • Workflow guide: Git Feature Workflow — Complete git workflow
  • Pattern guide: Interactive Commands Guide — How this pattern works

docs.title: Tutorial: Pre-Flight Checks with /craft:check
docs.text: Level: Beginner Time: 10 minutes Prerequisites: Craft plugin installed Version: 2.9.0+ (includes "Show Steps First" pattern)

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
By the end of this tutorial, you'll know how to:

  1. Run pre-flight checks before committing
  2. Understand the check output
  3. Use different modes (default vs thorough)
  4. Skip specific checks when needed
  5. Troubleshoot common failures

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
/craft:check is your safety net before committing code. It runs appropriate validation checks for your project type and tells you if something's wrong before you push broken code.

Think of it as:

  • ✈️ Pre-flight checklist for pilots
  • 🏥 Pre-surgery checklist for doctors
  • ✅ Pre-commit checklist for developers

docs.title: Step 1: Your First Check
docs.text:
Let's start with the simplest usage.

Run this command:

/craft:check

What happens (v2.9.0 "Show Steps First"):

╭─ Pre-Flight Validation Plan ─────────────────╮
│ Mode: default                                 │
│ Estimated time: 5-10 seconds                  │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                               │
│ Steps to run:                                 │
│                                               │
│ 1. Code Quality (2s)                          │
│    • markdownlint (24 rules)                  │
│    • python linting (ruff)                    │
│                                               │
│ 2. Quick Tests (3s)                           │
│    • Unit tests (fast subset, ~200 tests)     │
│                                               │
│ 3. Basic Validation (2s)                      │
│    • Config files                             │
│    • Required files                           │
│                                               │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Proceed with these checks? (y/n/switch-mode)  │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

What you see:

  • Mode: Which mode is running (default = quick checks)
  • Time estimate: How long it will take
  • Steps: What will be checked
  • Prompt: Choice to proceed, cancel, or switch modes

Your options:

  • Type y to proceed
  • Type n to cancel
  • Type switch-mode to use thorough mode instead

Try it: Type y and press Enter.

docs.title: Step 2: Understanding the Output
docs.text: After running, you'll see results like this:

docs.title: ✅ Successful Check
docs.text:
╭─ Pre-Flight Check Results ───────────────────╮
│                                               │
│ ✓ Code Quality (2.1s)                         │
│   • markdownlint: 45 files, 0 issues          │
│   • python linting: 12 files, 0 issues        │
│                                               │
│ ✓ Quick Tests (3.4s)                          │
│   • Unit tests: 203 passed, 0 failed          │
│                                               │
│ ✓ Basic Validation (1.8s)                     │
│   • Config files: valid                       │
│   • Required files: all present               │
│                                               │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Results: 3/3 checks passed                    │
│ Issues: 0 warnings, 0 errors                  │
│ Next steps: Ready to commit ✓                 │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

What this means:

  • ✓ Green checkmarks = everything passed
  • Ready to commit safely
  • All checks completed in ~7 seconds

What to do next:

git add .
git commit -m "feat: add new feature"
git push

docs.title: ❌ Failed Check
docs.text:
╭─ Pre-Flight Check Results ───────────────────╮
│                                               │
│ ✓ Code Quality (2.1s)                         │
│   • markdownlint: 44 files ok, 1 file failed  │
│   • python linting: 12 files, 0 issues        │
│                                               │
│ ✗ Quick Tests (2.8s)                          │
│   • Unit tests: 202 passed, 1 failed          │
│     Failed: test_auth_flow (line 45)          │
│     Error: AssertionError: Expected 200, got 401│
│                                               │
│ ✓ Basic Validation (1.8s)                     │
│   • Config files: valid                       │
│   • Required files: all present               │
│                                               │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Results: 2/3 checks passed                    │
│ Issues: 0 warnings, 2 errors                  │
│ Next steps: Fix errors before committing ✗    │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

What this means:

  • ✗ Red X = something failed
  • 2 errors found (1 markdown issue, 1 test failure)
  • DO NOT commit until fixed

What to do:

  1. Fix the markdown issue:
  2. See which file failed: check output

  3. Run auto-fix: npx markdownlint-cli2 --fix "**/*.md"

  4. Fix the test failure:

  5. Open the test file
  6. Check line 45 (test_auth_flow)

  7. Fix the assertion or the code being tested

  8. Re-run check:

/craft:check
  1. Commit only when all checks pass

docs.title: Step 3: Preview Mode (Dry Run)
docs.text:
Want to see what will be checked without actually running it?

Use dry-run mode:

/craft:check --dry-run

Output:

╭─ Pre-Flight Validation Plan (DRY RUN) ───────╮
│ Mode: default                                 │
│ Estimated time: 5-10 seconds                  │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                               │
│ Would run these steps:                        │
│                                               │
│ 1. Code Quality (2s)                          │
│    • markdownlint (24 rules)                  │
│    • python linting (ruff)                    │
│                                               │
│ 2. Quick Tests (3s)                           │
│    • Unit tests (fast subset, ~200 tests)     │
│                                               │
│ 3. Basic Validation (2s)                      │
│    • Config files                             │
│    • Required files                           │
│                                               │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ DRY RUN - No checks executed                  │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯

When to use:

  • First time using /craft:check (learn what it does)
  • Checking what mode will do
  • Planning your commit workflow

docs.title: Step 4: Mode Selection
docs.text: There are 2 modes available:

docs.title: Default Mode (Fast - 5-10 seconds)
docs.text:
/craft:check
# or explicitly:
/craft:check --mode=default

What it checks:

  • ✓ Basic code linting
  • ✓ Fast subset of tests
  • ✓ Config file syntax
  • ✓ Required files exist

Use for: Before every commit

docs.title: Thorough Mode (Comprehensive - 2-5 minutes)
docs.text:
/craft:check --mode=thorough

What it checks:

  • ✓ All linters with strict rules
  • ✓ Full test suite
  • ✓ Coverage analysis (85%+ threshold)
  • ✓ Broken link detection
  • ✓ Dependency security audit
  • ✓ Build verification

Use for:

  • Before creating PR
  • Before merging to main
  • Before releases
  • After major refactoring

docs.title: Mode Comparison
docs.text: Feature Default Thorough Time 5-10s 2-5 min Lint rules Basic Strict Tests Fast subset Full suite Coverage No Yes (85%+) Links No Yes Dependencies No Yes (security audit) Build No Yes Use case Every commit PR/Release

docs.title: Step 5: Skipping Checks
docs.text: Sometimes you want to skip specific checks.

docs.title: Skip Tests (Docs-Only Changes)
docs.text:
/craft:check --skip=tests

Use when:

  • Only changed documentation
  • Changed README or markdown files
  • No code changes

What runs:

  • ✓ Linting
  • ✗ Tests (skipped)
  • ✓ Validation

docs.title: Skip Multiple Checks
docs.text:
/craft:check --skip=lint,docs

Available skip options:

Flag Skips When to use --skip=lint Code/markdown linting Already ran linter manually --skip=tests Test execution Docs-only changes --skip=docs Documentation validation Code-only changes --skip=build Build verification Not ready to build yet --skip=deps Dependency audit No dependency changes

⚠️ Warning: Only skip checks when you're certain they're not needed. When in doubt, run all checks.

docs.title: Scenario 1: Quick Commit
docs.text:
# Make some changes
echo "# New Section" >> README.md

# Check
/craft:check

# If passes, commit
git add README.md
git commit -m "docs: add new section to README"
git push

Time: 6-10 seconds total

docs.title: Scenario 2: Before PR
docs.text:
# Feature complete, ready for review
git checkout feature/new-auth

# Run thorough check
/craft:check --mode=thorough

# If passes, create PR
gh pr create --base dev --title "Add JWT authentication"

Time: 2-5 minutes

docs.title: Scenario 3: Docs-Only Change
docs.text:
# Updated tutorials
git status
# modified: docs/tutorials/new-tutorial.md

# Check (skip tests since no code changed)
/craft:check --skip=tests

# Commit
git add docs/
git commit -m "docs: add new tutorial"
git push

Time: 3-5 seconds

docs.title: Issue 1: "Markdown lint failed"
docs.text:
Error:

✗ Code Quality
  • markdownlint: 1 file failed
    docs/guide.md:15:1 MD022 Headings should be surrounded by blank lines

Solution:

# Auto-fix markdown issues
npx markdownlint-cli2 --fix "docs/guide.md"

# Re-run check
/craft:check

docs.title: Issue 2: "Tests failed"
docs.text:
Error:

✗ Quick Tests
  • Unit tests: 1 failed
    test_api_auth: Expected 200, got 401

Solution:

  1. Open the test file
  2. Look at the failing test
  3. Fix either:
  4. The test (if test is wrong)
  5. The code (if code is wrong)
  6. Re-run: /craft:check

docs.title: Issue 3: "Coverage below threshold"
docs.text:
Error (thorough mode only):

✗ Coverage Analysis
  • Current: 82%
  • Threshold: 85%
  • Missing coverage in: src/auth.py

Solution:

# Add tests for uncovered code
# Then re-run
/craft:check --mode=thorough

docs.title: Issue 4: "Check takes too long"
docs.text:
Problem: Thorough mode takes 5+ minutes

Solutions:

# Option 1: Use default mode for quick feedback
/craft:check

# Option 2: Skip heavy checks
/craft:check --mode=thorough --skip=tests

# Option 3: Run only specific checks later
/craft:test              # Just tests
/craft:docs:check-links      # Just link validation

docs.title: ✅ DO
docs.text:
• Run /craft:check before every commit
  • Use default mode for commits, thorough for PRs
  • Fix all errors before committing
  • Read the error messages (they tell you what's wrong)
  • Re-run after fixing issues

docs.title: ❌ DON'T
docs.text:
• Skip checks to save time (you'll regret it later)
  • Commit when checks fail (breaks the build)
  • Ignore warnings (they often become errors)
  • Use --skip unless you're sure (easy to miss issues)

docs.title: Workflow Integration
docs.text:
Good workflow:

# 1. Make changes
<edit files>

# 2. Check
/craft:check

# 3. If fails, fix and repeat
<fix issues>
/craft:check

# 4. When passes, commit
git add .
git commit -m "message"
git push

Bad workflow:

# ❌ Don't do this
git add .
git commit -m "message"
git push
# <build breaks, CI fails, teammates angry>

docs.title: Step 9: Interactive Practice
docs.text: Try these exercises to build muscle memory:

docs.title: Exercise 1: First Check
docs.text:
# Run your first check
/craft:check

# Questions:
# - What mode ran?
# - How long did it take?
# - Did it pass?

docs.title: Exercise 2: Dry Run
docs.text:
# See the plan without running
/craft:check --dry-run

# Questions:
# - How many steps would run?
# - What's the estimated time?

docs.title: Exercise 3: Mode Comparison
docs.text:
# Run both modes
/craft:check --mode=default
/craft:check --mode=thorough --dry-run

# Questions:
# - What's the time difference?
# - What extra checks does thorough include?

docs.title: Exercise 4: Skip Practice
docs.text:
# Skip tests
/craft:check --skip=tests

# Questions:
# - Did it run faster?
# - What still ran?
# - When would you use this?

docs.title: Step 10: Next Steps
docs.text: You now know how to use /craft:check! Here's what to learn next:

docs.title: Immediate Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Add to workflow: Run /craft:check before your next commit
  2. Set up automation: Add pre-commit hook (optional)
  3. Explore modes: Try thorough mode before your next PR

docs.title: Further Learning
docs.text:
• Command mastery: Check Command Mastery Guide
  • Quick reference: Check Command Reference
  • Cookbook recipe: Check Before Commit
  • All commands: Commands Overview

docs.title: Advanced Topics
docs.text:
• Custom validators: Add project-specific checks
  • CI integration: Use in GitHub Actions
  • Mode customization: Configure coverage thresholds
  • Auto-fix workflows: Automate common fixes

docs.title: Essential Commands
docs.text:
# Basic usage
/craft:check                     # Run default mode

# Preview mode
/craft:check --dry-run           # Show plan without running

# Mode selection
/craft:check --mode=default      # Fast (5-10s)
/craft:check --mode=thorough     # Comprehensive (2-5min)

# Skip checks
/craft:check --skip=tests        # Skip test execution
/craft:check --skip=lint,docs    # Skip multiple

docs.title: Decision Tree
docs.text:
Before committing?
  → /craft:check

Before PR?
  → /craft:check --mode=thorough

Docs only?
  → /craft:check --skip=tests

Want to preview?
  → /craft:check --dry-run

docs.title: Troubleshooting Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
Error Solution Markdown lint failed npx markdownlint-cli2 --fix "**/*.md" Tests failed Fix the test or code, re-run check Coverage too low Add more tests Checks too slow Use --skip or default mode

Congratulations! You've completed the /craft:check beginner tutorial. You're now ready to validate your code before committing.

Time to complete: ~10 minutes Commands learned: 5 Next tutorial: Smart Routing with /craft:do

Last Updated: 2026-01-29 (v2.9.0) Tutorial Version: 1.0 Prerequisites: Craft v2.9.0+

docs.title: Tutorial: CLAUDE.md Management Workflows
docs.text:
Complete guide to creating, maintaining, and optimizing CLAUDE.md files with craft's claude-md command suite.

Table of Contents:

  1. Getting Started
  2. Workflow 1: New Project Setup
  3. Workflow 2: Maintenance & Updates
  4. Workflow 3: Section Editing
  5. Workflow 4: Template Customization
  6. Workflow 5: Layered Architecture
  7. Advanced Patterns
  8. Troubleshooting

docs.title: What is CLAUDE.md?
docs.text:
CLAUDE.md is a knowledge file that provides Claude Code with project-specific context:

  • Project structure and key files
  • Custom commands and workflows
  • Development conventions
  • Build/test/deployment instructions
  • Current status and progress

docs.title: The claude-md Command Suite
docs.text:
Craft provides specialized commands for CLAUDE.md management:

Command Purpose Interactive /craft:docs:claude-md:init Create from template Yes /craft:docs:claude-md:sync Unified sync pipeline (validate + update + fix) Yes /craft:docs:claude-md:edit Section-by-section editing Yes

Note: scaffold -> init, update/audit/fix -> sync (consolidated in v2.12.0, old aliases removed in v2.13.0).

All commands follow the "Show Steps First" pattern - preview before executing.

docs.title: Workflow 1: New Project Setup
docs.text: Goal: Create a well-structured CLAUDE.md from scratch in 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1: Navigate to Project
docs.text: cd ~/projects/dev-tools/my-craft-plugin

docs.title: Step 2: Scaffold from Template
docs.text:
/craft:docs:claude-md:init

What happens:

  1. Project Type Detection
Detecting project type...

Found indicators:
  .claude-plugin/plugin.json  (craft plugin)
  commands/ directory         (51 commands)
  skills/ directory           (8 skills)
  agents/ directory           (3 agents)
  tests/ directory            (127 test files)

Detected: craft-plugin

Template: plugin-template.md
  1. Variable Auto-Population
Extracting variables from project...

From plugin.json:
  name: my-craft-plugin
  version: 1.0.0
  description: Development workflow automation

From filesystem:
  commands: 51
  skills: 8
  agents: 3
  tests: 127

From git:
  repo: github.com/user/my-craft-plugin
  branch: main

Auto-populated: 18/18 variables (100%)
  1. Preview Generated File
Preview: CLAUDE.md (242 lines)

# CLAUDE.md - My Craft Plugin

> Development workflow automation

**51 commands** · **8 skills** · **3 agents** · [Docs](https://user.github.io/my-craft-plugin)

## Quick Commands

| Task | Shell | Craft |
|------|-------|-------|
| Run tests | `python3 -m pytest` | `/craft:test` |
| Lint code | `ruff check .` | `/craft:code:lint` |
...

  Proceed with file creation?
  > Yes - Create CLAUDE.md (Recommended)
    Customize - Edit before saving
    Dry run - Show full content without creating
    Cancel - Exit without changes
  1. Confirmation and Creation
Created: CLAUDE.md (242 lines)

Summary:
  18 variables populated automatically
  3 sections: Quick Commands, Project Structure, Git Workflow
  0 TODOs (all auto-filled)

Next steps:
  1. Review: cat CLAUDE.md
  2. Customize: /craft:docs:claude-md:edit
  3. Validate: /craft:docs:claude-md:sync

docs.title: Step 3: Customize Key Sections
docs.text:
/craft:docs:claude-md:edit

Interactive section selection:

CLAUDE.md sections (8 total):

1. Quick Commands (12 commands)
2. Project Structure (5 directories)
3. Git Workflow (3 sections)
4. Development (2 sections)
5. Testing (1 section)
6. Troubleshooting (5 items)
7. Integration (2 sections)
8. Links (4 links)

Which section to edit? (1-8, all, cancel): 1

Edit in preferred editor:

Opening section: Quick Commands

Editor: iA Writer (default)
Tip: Use -e code flag for VS Code

[Opens in iA Writer...]

When done:
  - Save file (⌘S)
  - Return to terminal
  - Press Enter

docs.title: Step 4: Validate
docs.text:
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

Validation results:

AUDIT RESULTS

File: CLAUDE.md (242 lines)
Last modified: 30 seconds ago

Checks:
  Version sync         (from plugin.json)
  Command coverage     (51/51 documented)
  Required sections    (8/8 present)
  Broken links         (0 found)
  Template compliance  (matches plugin-template)

Results:
  0 errors
  0 warnings
  1 info (optimization available)

Status: PASSED

docs.title: Step 5: Commit
docs.text:
git add CLAUDE.md
git commit -m "docs: add CLAUDE.md with auto-populated template"

Complete workflow time: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Workflow 2: Maintenance and Updates
docs.text: Goal: Keep CLAUDE.md current as project evolves.

docs.title: When to Run
docs.text:
• After adding/removing commands
  • After version bumps
  • Before releases
  • Weekly maintenance

docs.title: Step 1: Check Current Status
docs.text:
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

Typical results after active development:

AUDIT RESULTS

File: CLAUDE.md (242 lines)
Last modified: 2 weeks ago

Issues Found:

ERRORS (2):
  Line 45: Command `/craft:old-command` no longer exists
  Line 78: Broken link to docs/removed-guide.md

WARNINGS (3):
  Version mismatch: CLAUDE.md says v1.0.0, plugin.json says v1.2.0
  Progress drift: CLAUDE.md says 60%, .STATUS says 85%
  Missing commands: 3 new commands not documented

INFO (1):
  File length: 242 lines (optimization recommended if > 300)

docs.title: Step 2: Auto-Fix Common Issues
docs.text:
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --fix

Fix process:

Auto-fixing issues...

Fix 1/5: Remove stale command reference
  Line 45: `/craft:old-command`
  Action: Remove from Quick Commands table
  Status: FIXED

Fix 2/5: Remove broken link
  Line 78: docs/removed-guide.md
  Action: Remove link reference
  Status: FIXED

Fix 3/5: Update version
  Current: v1.0.0
  Actual: v1.2.0 (from plugin.json)
  Action: Update version number
  Status: FIXED

Fix 4/5: Sync progress
  Current: 60%
  Actual: 85% (from .STATUS)
  Action: Update progress percentage
  Status: FIXED

Fix 5/5: Add new commands (MANUAL)
  Missing: /craft:new-feature, /craft:validate, /craft:analyze
  Action: Use /craft:docs:claude-md:sync to add
  Status: SKIPPED (manual)

Summary:
  4 fixed automatically
  1 requires manual action

docs.title: Step 3: Update with New Commands
docs.text:
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

Update process with preview:

CLAUDE.md Update Plan

Changes Detected:

1. Version Sync (already fixed)
   v1.0.0 → v1.2.0

2. New Commands (3)
   + /craft:new-feature
   + /craft:validate
   + /craft:analyze

3. Updated Metrics
   Commands: 51 → 54 (+3)
   Tests: 127 → 145 (+18)

4. Documentation Status
   Current: 85% complete
   Actual: 92% complete (+7%)

Net changes: +15 lines, -0 lines

  Proceed with updates?
  > Yes - Apply all changes (Recommended)
    Interactive - Choose which sections to update
    Dry run - Show preview without applying
    Cancel - Exit without changes

After applying:

CLAUDE.MD UPDATED

Applied:
  Version synced: v1.0.0 → v1.2.0
  Added 3 new commands to Quick Commands table
  Updated command count: 51 → 54
  Updated test count: 127 → 145
  Updated documentation status: 85% → 92%

File changes:
  Lines: 242 → 257 (+15)
  Sections: 8 (unchanged)

Post-Update Validation:
  All required sections present
  Version matches plugin.json
  All commands documented
  No broken links detected

Next steps:
  1. Review: git diff CLAUDE.md
  2. Commit: git add CLAUDE.md && git commit -m "docs: update CLAUDE.md for v1.2.0"

docs.title: Step 4: Verify and Commit
docs.text:
# Review changes
git diff CLAUDE.md

# Verify with audit
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

# Commit
git add CLAUDE.md
git commit -m "docs: update CLAUDE.md for v1.2.0"

Complete workflow time: ~3 minutes

docs.title: Workflow 3: Section Editing
docs.text: Goal: Edit specific sections without touching entire file.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Update troubleshooting tips
  • Add integration notes
  • Refine command descriptions

docs.text:

Updated:
  CLAUDE.md (257 → 259 lines)

Backup created: .CLAUDE.md.backup

Validation:
  Structure intact
  No broken formatting
  All sections present

Next steps:
  1. Review full file: cat CLAUDE.md
  2. Edit another section: /craft:docs:claude-md:edit
  3. Validate: /craft:docs:claude-md:sync
  4. Commit: git add CLAUDE.md && git commit -m "docs: expand troubleshooting section"

Complete workflow time: ~2 minutes per section

docs.title: Workflow 4: Template Customization
docs.text: Goal: Customize templates for organization-specific needs.

docs.title: When to Customize
docs.text:
• Organization has standard CLAUDE.md structure
  • Need project-type-specific sections
  • Want consistent branding/formatting

docs.title: Templates Location
docs.text:
ls templates/claude-md/

Output:

plugin-template.md        (craft plugins)
teaching-template.md      (Quarto course sites)
r-package-template.md     (R packages)

docs.title: Step 1: Copy Template
docs.text:
# Copy existing template as starting point
cp templates/claude-md/plugin-template.md templates/claude-md/my-org-plugin.md

docs.title: Step 2: Edit Template
docs.text:
# Open in editor
code templates/claude-md/my-org-plugin.md

Template structure with variables:

# CLAUDE.md - {{plugin_name}}

> {{tagline}}

**{{command_count}} commands** · **{{skill_count}} skills** · **{{agent_count}} agents**

## Quick Commands

{{command_table}}

## Organization Standards

### Code Review Process

All changes require:
- [ ] PR review by 2+ team members
- [ ] CI passing (lint + tests + security)
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] CHANGELOG entry added

### Deployment Workflow

...

Available variables:

  • {{plugin_name}} - From plugin.json
  • {{version}} - From plugin.json
  • {{tagline}} - From plugin.json description
  • {{command_count}} - Auto-detected
  • {{skill_count}} - Auto-detected
  • {{agent_count}} - Auto-detected
  • {{test_count}} - Auto-detected
  • {{command_table}} - Auto-generated table
  • {{repo_url}} - From git remote
  • {{docs_url}} - From plugin.json

docs.title: Step 3: Use Custom Template
docs.text:
Option 1: Default for project type

Rename to match project type detection:

mv templates/claude-md/my-org-plugin.md templates/claude-md/craft-plugin.md

Option 2: Explicit template selection

# Force template when scaffolding
/craft:docs:claude-md:init --template=my-org-plugin

docs.title: Step 4: Test Template
docs.text:
# Test in a sample project
cd ~/projects/test-plugin
/craft:docs:claude-md:init

# Verify variables populated correctly
cat CLAUDE.md

Complete workflow time: ~15-30 minutes (one-time setup)

docs.title: Workflow 5: Layered Architecture
docs.text:
Goal: Keep CLAUDE.md lean (~80 lines) by separating behavioral rules from reference material.

v2.22.0: CLAUDE.md now uses a layered system that loads only behavioral rules every session. Reference material lives in .claude/reference/ and loads on-demand.

docs.title: The Principle
docs.text: Layer Loads Contains Budget CLAUDE.md Every session Behavioral rules, workflow, troubleshooting < 100 lines .claude/rules/ Every session Imperatives (spec-only, brainstorm) As needed .claude/reference/ On-demand Agents, tests, project structure Unlimited

docs.title: Step 1: Generate Reference Files
docs.text:
# Auto-generate from filesystem state
PYTHONPATH=. python3 utils/claude_md_sync.py --generate-reference

This creates:

  • .claude/reference/agents.md — Agent inventory with model and description
  • .claude/reference/test-suite.md — Test files with type classification
  • .claude/reference/project-structure.md — Directory tree, counts, version

docs.title: Step 2: Keep CLAUDE.md Behavioral
docs.text:
CLAUDE.md should answer "how should Claude behave?" not "what tools exist?"

Keep in CLAUDE.md:

  • Git workflow rules and constraints
  • Quick command reference (what to run)
  • Execution modes
  • Troubleshooting

Move to .claude/reference/:

  • Agent tables with descriptions
  • Full test file listings
  • Project structure trees
  • Version history

docs.title: Step 3: Auto-Maintain with Workflow
docs.text:
# Session end: auto-syncs counts and timestamps
/workflow:done

# Pre-flight: validates instruction health
/craft:check

Both commands now include CLAUDE.md accuracy checks automatically.

docs.title: Pattern 1: Dry-Run Before Commit
docs.text:
Always preview changes before committing:

# Update with dry-run first
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --dry-run

# Review what would change
# Then apply if satisfied
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

docs.title: Pattern 2: Interactive Section Updates
docs.text:
Selectively update only changed sections:

/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --interactive

Prompts for each section:

Section: Quick Commands (3 new commands detected)
  Add: /craft:new-feature, /craft:validate, /craft:analyze
  Apply? (y/n/skip): y

Section: Project Structure (no changes)
  Apply? (y/n/skip): skip

Section: Testing (test count changed)
  Update: 127 → 145 tests
  Apply? (y/n/skip): y

docs.title: Pattern 3: Optimization Pass
docs.text:
Condense verbose CLAUDE.md files:

# Update and optimize in one step
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --optimize

Optimization actions:

OPTIMIZATION APPLIED

Condensed:
  Quick Commands: Shortened verbose descriptions
  Project Structure: Grouped related directories
  Code examples: Removed obvious comments

Before: 287 lines
After: 231 lines (-56 lines, -19%)

Quality maintained:
  All essential info preserved
  All links still valid
  Structure unchanged

docs.title: Pattern 4: Pre-Release Validation
docs.text:
Before releases, ensure CLAUDE.md is pristine:

# Comprehensive check
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --strict

# Fix any issues
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --fix

# Update to latest
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

# Final validation
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --strict

docs.title: Pattern 5: Cross-Project Consistency
docs.text:
For monorepo or multiple related projects:

Create shared base template:

# templates/claude-md/org-base.md

## Organization Standards

[Shared content across all projects...]

## Project-Specific

{{project_specific_content}}

Inherit in project templates:

# templates/claude-md/plugin-template.md

{{include:org-base.md}}

## Plugin-Specific Sections

...

docs.title: Pattern 6: Git Worktree Integration
docs.text:
When creating feature branches with worktrees:

# Create worktree
git worktree add ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-xyz -b feature/xyz dev

# Navigate to worktree
cd ~/.git-worktrees/craft/feature-xyz

# If no CLAUDE.md, scaffold offers to create
/craft:docs:claude-md:init

# During development, keep updated
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

# Before merging, validate
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --strict

docs.title: Issue: "Could not detect project type"
docs.text:
Symptoms:

/craft:docs:claude-md:init

Error: Could not detect project type
No valid project indicators found

Solutions:

  1. Check for indicator files:
# Craft plugin
ls .claude-plugin/plugin.json

# Teaching site
ls _quarto.yml course.yml

# R package
ls DESCRIPTION NAMESPACE
  1. Force template:
/craft:docs:claude-md:init --template=plugin
  1. Use generic template:
/craft:docs:claude-md:init --template=generic

docs.title: Issue: "Version mismatch not auto-fixing"
docs.text:
Symptoms:

/craft:docs:claude-md:sync --fix

Warning: Version mismatch
  CLAUDE.md: v1.0.0
  plugin.json: v1.2.0
  Status: NOT FIXED (file not found)

Solutions:

  1. Check version source exists:
# For plugins
cat .claude-plugin/plugin.json | grep version

# For Node
cat package.json | grep version

# For Python
cat pyproject.toml | grep version
  1. Manual update:
# Edit directly
/craft:docs:claude-md:edit

# Or use update command
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync status

docs.title: Issue: "New commands not being added"
docs.text:
Symptoms:

/craft:docs:claude-md:sync

Detected 3 new commands:
  /craft:new-feature
  /craft:validate
  /craft:analyze

But Quick Commands table not updated

Solutions:

  1. Check command detection:
# Verify commands exist
ls commands/new-feature.md
ls commands/validate.md
ls commands/analyze.md
  1. Check frontmatter:
# Each command file needs frontmatter
---
description: Command description
category: code
---
  1. Force full update:
/craft:docs:claude-md:sync commands

docs.title: Issue: "Section editing changes entire file"
docs.text:
Symptoms:

/craft:docs:claude-md:edit

Selected section: Quick Commands
But diff shows changes across entire file

Solutions:

  1. Check section headers:
# Must use top-level headers (##) for sections
## Quick Commands   ← Correct
### Quick Commands  ← Wrong (subsection)
  1. Verify section boundaries:
# List all sections
grep "^## " CLAUDE.md
  1. Re-scaffold if structure broken:
# Backup first
cp CLAUDE.md CLAUDE.md.manual-backup

# Re-scaffold
/craft:docs:claude-md:init --force

# Merge manual changes back

docs.title: Issue: "Template variables not populating"
docs.text:
Symptoms:

/craft:docs:claude-md:init

Created CLAUDE.md with TODOs:
  {{plugin_name}} not populated
  {{version}} not populated

Solutions:

  1. Check source files exist:
# For plugin template
cat .claude-plugin/plugin.json

# For teaching template
cat _quarto.yml

# For R package template
cat DESCRIPTION
  1. Check file format:
// plugin.json must be valid JSON
{
  "name": "my-plugin",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Plugin description"
}
  1. Manual population:
# Edit template variables directly
/craft:docs:claude-md:edit

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Command Reference: CLAUDE-MD Commands
  • Quick Reference: REFCARD-CLAUDE-MD
  • Interactive Commands Guide: Interactive Commands
  • Templates: templates/claude-md/

Tutorial Version: 1.1.0 Last Updated: 2026-02-19 Craft Version: v2.22.0+

docs.title: Getting Started with Dependency Management
docs.text:
Level: Beginner Time: ~10 minutes Prerequisites: None

Learn how to use the /craft:docs:demo dependency management system to check, install, and manage tools for terminal GIF generation.

docs.title: Tutorial Overview
docs.text:
By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to:

  • ✅ Check which tools are installed
  • ✅ Understand dependency status
  • ✅ Install missing tools automatically
  • ✅ Convert .cast files to optimized GIFs
  • ✅ Troubleshoot common issues

docs.title: Learning Path
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Start] --> B[Check Dependencies]
    B --> C{All Tools<br/>Installed?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Convert Demo]
    C -->|No| E[Install Missing]
    E --> F[Verify Install]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Done!]

    style A fill:#4CAF50
    style G fill:#4CAF50
    style C fill:#FF9800

docs.title: Step 1: Verify the System Exists
docs.text:
Before starting, let's verify the dependency management system is available.

What you'll do:

# Check that the demo command exists
ls -la commands/docs/demo.md

Expected output:

-rw-r--r-- 1 user staff 15234 Jan 17 demo.md

What this means:

  • The /craft:docs:demo command is installed
  • The dependency management system is ready to use

docs.title: Step 2: Check Your Current Dependencies
docs.text:
Let's see what tools you already have installed.

Command:

/craft:docs:demo --check

What happens:

  1. Reads tool specifications from YAML frontmatter
  2. Detects which tools are installed
  3. Checks tool health and versions
  4. Displays a formatted status table

Example output:

╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  DEPENDENCY STATUS - asciinema method              ║
╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Tool         Status    Version    Health    Required
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
asciinema    ✅        2.3.0      ok        yes
agg          ❌        -          n/a       yes
gifsicle     ✅        1.96       ok        yes
fswatch      ⚠️        -          n/a       no

Summary: 2/3 required tools ready

Understanding the status:

  • ✅ Green checkmark: Tool installed and working
  • ❌ Red X: Required tool missing
  • ⚠️ Warning: Optional tool missing (not critical)

docs.title: Step 3: Understand the Methods
docs.text: The system supports two methods for creating GIFs:

docs.title: Method 1: asciinema (Recommended)
docs.text:
Best for: Real terminal recordings, authentic output

Required tools:

  • asciinema - Record terminal sessions
  • agg - Convert .cast to .gif
  • gifsicle - Optimize GIF size

Optional:

  • fswatch - Watch mode for live updates

Check this method:

/craft:docs:demo --check --method asciinema

docs.title: Method 2: VHS
docs.text:
Best for: Scripted demos, repeatable workflows

Required tools:

  • vhs - Generate scripted demos
  • gifsicle - Optimize GIF size

Optional:

  • ffmpeg - Video processing

Check this method:

/craft:docs:demo --check --method vhs

💡 Tip: Most users start with asciinema for real recordings.

docs.title: Step 4: Install Missing Dependencies
docs.text:
If you have missing tools, the system can install them automatically.

Command:

/craft:docs:demo --fix

What happens:

  1. Detects which tools are missing
  2. Asks for your consent to install each tool
  3. Tries multiple installation methods:
  4. Homebrew (macOS)
  5. Cargo (Rust)
  6. Binary download
  7. APT/YUM (Linux)
  8. Verifies each installation succeeded

Example interaction:

Missing dependencies detected:
  - agg (required for asciinema method)

Install agg via cargo? (y/n): y

Installing agg via cargo...
✓ agg installed successfully

Verifying installation...
✓ agg version 1.4.3 detected
✓ Health check passed

All dependencies installed!

Installation safety:

  • ✅ Always asks for consent
  • ✅ Tries multiple methods
  • ✅ Verifies after install
  • ✅ Reports any failures

docs.title: Step 5: Verify Installation Success
docs.text:
After installation, check that everything is working.

Command:

/craft:docs:demo --check

Expected output (all green):

╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  DEPENDENCY STATUS - asciinema method              ║
╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Tool         Status    Version    Health    Required
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
asciinema    ✅        2.3.0      ok        yes
agg          ✅        1.4.3      ok        yes
gifsicle     ✅        1.96       ok        yes

Summary: All required tools ready ✨

If you still see issues:

  • See Step 9: Troubleshooting
  • Check DEPENDENCY-MANAGEMENT.md

docs.title: Step 5.5: Migrating from VHS (Optional)
docs.text: If you were using VHS before, here's how to migrate to the asciinema workflow:

docs.title: Why Migrate?
docs.text:
The asciinema method offers several advantages:

  • ✅ Real terminal capture (actual TTY, not scripted)
  • ✅ Smaller file sizes (text-based .cast files)
  • ✅ Faster conversion (agg is optimized for .cast → .gif)
  • ✅ Better accuracy (no timing/rendering issues)
  • ✅ Easier editing (.cast files are JSON, VHS tapes are scripts)

docs.title: Migration Steps
docs.text:
1. Verify asciinema method is ready:

/craft:docs:demo --check
# Should show all asciinema tools installed

2. Re-record your demos using asciinema:

# Instead of writing a VHS tape file:
# vhs my-demo.tape

# Use asciinema for real terminal capture:
asciinema rec my-demo.cast
# <perform your demo commands>
# <press Ctrl+D when done>

3. Convert with the new workflow:

# Old VHS workflow:
# vhs my-demo.tape   # Generates my-demo.gif

# New asciinema workflow:
/craft:docs:demo --convert my-demo.cast
# Generates my-demo.gif using agg + gifsicle

docs.title: Key Differences
docs.text: Feature VHS (Old) asciinema (New) Input Scripted .tape files Real terminal .cast files Recording Write commands in file Record live session Timing Predefined in script Actual command timing Editing Edit .tape script Edit JSON .cast file Output Direct to .gif .cast → .gif conversion File size Larger (~500KB-2MB) Smaller (~50KB-200KB)

docs.title: Handling Existing VHS Files
docs.text:
Keep VHS as fallback:

# VHS is still available via --method flag
/craft:docs:demo --method vhs my-old-tape.tape

# Or if you prefer VHS for specific demos:
vhs my-scripted-demo.tape

Gradual migration:

  • Keep existing VHS demos if they work well
  • Use asciinema for new demos going forward
  • Re-record critical demos when you have time

docs.title: Troubleshooting Migration
docs.text:
Issue: "I prefer scripted demos for consistency"

  • Solution: Use asciinema cat to replay .cast files consistently
  • Or: Keep VHS for scripted demos, use asciinema for real demos

Issue: "My .cast files are too long"

  • Solution: Use asciinema play --speed 2 to preview at 2x speed
  • Or: Edit .cast JSON to adjust timing (see asciinema docs)

Issue: "I want the old VHS styling"

  • Solution: agg supports custom themes via --theme flag
  • Or: Use gifsicle to adjust colors/palette

docs.title: Step 6: Create Your First Recording
docs.text:
Now let's record a simple terminal session.

Record a demo:

# Record a session (press Ctrl+D when done)
asciinema rec my-first-demo.cast

# Inside the recording, try some commands:
echo "Hello, World!"
ls -la
pwd

# Exit the recording
<Ctrl+D>

What you'll see:

asciinema: recording started, press <Ctrl-D> or type "exit" to finish
$ echo "Hello, World!"
Hello, World!
$ ls -la
...
$ pwd
/Users/you/project
$ <Ctrl+D>
asciinema: recording finished

Output: my-first-demo.cast file created

docs.title: Step 7: Convert Recording to GIF
docs.text:
Convert your .cast recording to an optimized GIF.

Command:

/craft:docs:demo --convert my-first-demo.cast

What happens:

  1. Validates the .cast file
  2. Converts using agg (with optimized settings)
  3. Optimizes with gifsicle
  4. Reports statistics

Expected output:

Converting: my-first-demo.cast → my-first-demo.gif

✓ Conversion successful
  Duration: 2.3s
  Size: 145KB
  Output: my-first-demo.gif

Conversion settings:

  • Font size: 16
  • Line height: 1.4
  • Theme: monokai
  • Optimization: Level 3 (maximum)
  • Colors: 256

View your GIF:

open my-first-demo.gif

docs.title: Step 8: Batch Convert Multiple Files
docs.text:
If you have many .cast files, convert them all at once.

Scenario: You have multiple recordings

# Create demo recordings
docs/
  demo1.cast
  demo2.cast
  demo3.cast

Batch conversion:

/craft:docs:demo --batch

What happens:

  1. Finds all .cast files in search path (default: docs/)
  2. Filters out files with existing .gif (unless --force)
  3. Converts each file with progress tracking
  4. Displays summary

Example output:

Found 3 .cast files

Converting batch...
[████████░░] 2/3 (67%) - ETA: 8s

✓ demo1.cast → demo1.gif (2.1s, 145KB)
✓ demo2.cast → demo2.gif (1.8s, 98KB)
✓ demo3.cast → demo3.gif (3.2s, 187KB)

Summary: 3/3 converted successfully
Total time: 15.4s
Total size: 430KB

Batch options:

# Search different directory
/craft:docs:demo --batch --search-path examples/

# Overwrite existing GIFs
/craft:docs:demo --batch --force

# Preview without converting
/craft:docs:demo --batch --dry-run

docs.title: Step 9: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and solutions.

docs.title: Issue 1: Tool Not Detected (Installed but Shows Missing)
docs.text:
Symptom:

agg          ❌        -          n/a       yes

Solutions:

# Verify tool is in PATH
which agg

# Reload shell
exec $SHELL

# Check PATH
echo $PATH

docs.title: Issue 2: Installation Failed
docs.text:
Symptom:

Installing agg via cargo... ✗ Failed

Solutions:

# Try manual installation
cargo install agg

# Or download binary
curl -L https://github.com/asciinema/agg/releases/latest/download/agg-x86_64-apple-darwin -o /usr/local/bin/agg
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/agg

docs.title: Issue 3: Health Check Failing
docs.text:
Symptom:

asciinema    ⚠️        2.3.0      broken    yes

Solutions:

# Test tool manually
asciinema --version

# Reinstall
brew uninstall asciinema
/craft:docs:demo --fix

docs.title: Issue 4: GIF Too Large
docs.text:
Symptom:

✓ demo.cast → demo.gif (5.2s, 2.4MB)

Solutions:

# Further optimize
gifsicle -O3 --colors 128 demo.gif -o demo-small.gif

# Reduce recording length
# Record shorter sessions

# Use lower color count
gifsicle -O2 --colors 64 demo.gif -o demo-minimal.gif

docs.title: Step 10: Next Steps
docs.text:
Congratulations! You've completed the getting started tutorial. 🎉

What you've learned:

  • ✅ Check dependency status
  • ✅ Install missing tools
  • ✅ Record terminal sessions
  • ✅ Convert .cast to .gif
  • ✅ Batch process recordings
  • ✅ Troubleshoot common issues

Continue learning:

docs.title: Intermediate Topics
docs.text:
• Advanced conversion: Custom agg settings, themes, optimization
  • CI/CD integration: Automate dependency checks in GitHub Actions
  • VHS scripting: Create repeatable scripted demos
  • Watch mode: Iterative development with live updates

Resources:

  • User Guide - Complete reference
  • API Reference - Function documentation
  • Architecture - System design
  • Developer Guide - Contributing

docs.title: Try These Commands
docs.text:
Get JSON output for scripting:

/craft:docs:demo --check --json

Check specific tool versions:

./scripts/version-check.sh validate asciinema

Detect repair candidates:

./scripts/repair-tools.sh detect asciinema

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Task Command Check all dependencies /craft:docs:demo --check Check specific method /craft:docs:demo --check --method asciinema Install missing tools /craft:docs:demo --fix Record terminal asciinema rec demo.cast Convert single file /craft:docs:demo --convert demo.cast Batch convert /craft:docs:demo --batch Get JSON output /craft:docs:demo --check --json

docs.title: Feedback
docs.text:
Did this tutorial help you? Have suggestions for improvement?

  • Open an issue on GitHub
  • Submit improvements via PR
  • Share your feedback in discussions

Last Updated: 2026-01-17 Version: 1.26.0 Tutorial Duration: ~10 minutes Completion Rate Target: 80%+

docs.title: Tutorial: Interactive Documentation Updates
docs.text:
Learn how to use /craft:docs:update --interactive to fix documentation issues across your project.

Time: 10-15 minutes Difficulty: Beginner Prerequisites: craft plugin installed

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to detect documentation issues automatically
  • How to use interactive prompts to choose what to fix
  • How to preview changes before applying them
  • How to update specific categories independently

docs.title: Why Interactive Mode?
docs.text:
Traditional documentation updates require:

  1. Manually finding issues
  2. Deciding what to fix
  3. Editing files one by one
  4. Hoping you didn't break anything

Interactive mode does all of this automatically while giving you control at each step.

docs.title: Tutorial Overview
docs.text:
Step 1: Preview (Dry-Run)        [5 min]
Step 2: Interactive Update       [5 min]
Step 3: Verify Changes           [3 min]
Step 4: Category-Specific Mode   [3 min]

docs.title: Step 1: Preview What Would Change (Dry-Run)
docs.text: First, let's see what documentation issues exist without making any changes.

docs.title: Run the Command
docs.text: /craft:docs:update --interactive --dry-run

docs.title: What Happens
docs.text:
The system runs two detection utilities:

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ DETECTING CHANGES                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                 │
│ Running detection utilities...                  │
│   ✓ docs_detector.py - Scanned 9 categories     │
│   ✓ help_file_validator.py - Validated help     │
│                                                 │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

docs.title: Detection Results
docs.text:
You'll see issues grouped by category:

Found issues in 6 categories:

  📦 Version References        545 items
  📊 Command Counts            289 items
  📝 Missing Help               60 items
  🏷️  Outdated Status            27 items
  📖 Inconsistent Terminology   44 items
  🔗 Missing Cross-References  366 items

Categories with no issues:
  ✓ Broken Links
  ✓ Stale Examples
  ✓ Outdated Diagrams

docs.title: Interactive Prompts (Preview)
docs.text:
You'll be prompted for each category group:

Prompt 1: Metadata Updates

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Version References (545 items)                  │
│ Should I update version references?             │
│                                                 │
│   A) Yes, update all (545 files → v2.5.1)      │
│   B) Preview changes first                      │
│   C) Skip this category                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Try selecting different options to see what each does:

  • Option A: Shows what would be updated
  • Option B: Shows detailed file list
  • Option C: Skips to next category

docs.title: Preview Summary
docs.text:
At the end, you'll see:

DRY RUN COMPLETE
Summary of Changes (No files modified)

Total Changes: 1,331 updates across 6 categories
Files That Would Be Modified: ~180 files

To apply these changes (without --dry-run):
  /craft:docs:update --interactive

Key Point: Nothing was changed! This was just a preview.

docs.title: Step 2: Apply Updates Interactively
docs.text: Now let's actually fix some documentation issues.

docs.title: Run the Command (No --dry-run)
docs.text: /craft:docs:update --interactive

docs.title: Choose What to Fix
docs.text:
This time, your selections will actually apply updates.

Example Session:

Prompt 1: Version References
  Your answer: A (Yes, update all)
  ✓ Updated 545 version references

Prompt 2: Command Counts
  Your answer: A (Yes, update all)
  ✓ Updated 289 command counts

Prompt 3: Missing Help
  Your answer: C (Skip this category)
  ⊘ Skipped by user

... (other categories)

docs.title: Watch It Work
docs.text:
As each category is applied, you'll see progress:

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ APPLYING UPDATES                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                 │
│ ✓ Version References (545 updates)              │
│   Updated files:                                │
│   • docs/VERSION-HISTORY.md (63 changes)        │
│   • README.md (35 changes)                      │
│   • CLAUDE.md (23 changes)                      │
│   ... and 64 more files                         │
│                                                 │
│ ✓ Command Counts (289 updates)                  │
│   Updated files:                                │
│   • CLAUDE.md (2 changes)                       │
│   • docs/architecture.md (4 changes)            │
│   ... and 30 more files                         │
│                                                 │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

docs.title: Final Summary
docs.text:
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ ✅ DOCUMENTATION UPDATE COMPLETE                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                 │
│ Categories Updated:                             │
│   ✓ Version references: 545 updates             │
│   ✓ Command counts: 289 updates                 │
│   ⊘ Missing help: Skipped                       │
│                                                 │
│ Files Modified: 67                              │
│ Total Changes: 834 updates                      │
│ Time: 3.2 seconds                               │
│                                                 │
│ Next Steps:                                     │
│   1. Review: git diff                           │
│   2. Test: /craft:test                      │
│   3. Commit: git add . && git commit            │
│                                                 │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Changes
docs.text: Always review what was changed before committing.

docs.title: Check What Files Changed
docs.text:
git status

Output:

modified:   README.md
modified:   CLAUDE.md
modified:   docs/VERSION-HISTORY.md
... (64 more files)

docs.title: Review Specific Changes
docs.text:
# See all changes
git diff

# See changes in one file
git diff README.md

# Count changes
git diff --stat

docs.title: Example Diff
docs.text:
- **Version:** v1.24.0
+ **Version:** v2.5.1

- craft has 99 commands
+ craft has 101 commands

docs.title: If Everything Looks Good
docs.text:
git add .
git commit -m "docs: update version refs and counts via interactive mode"

docs.title: If You Made a Mistake
docs.text:
# Undo all changes
git checkout .

# Undo changes in one file
git checkout README.md

# Then run interactive mode again

docs.title: Step 4: Category-Specific Updates
docs.text: Sometimes you only want to fix one type of issue.

docs.title: Update Just Version References
docs.text:
/craft:docs:update --interactive --category=version_refs

Benefits:

  • Faster (only scans one category)
  • Focused (easier to review changes)
  • Safer (less chance of unexpected changes)

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Category-Specific Mode: version_refs            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                 │
│ Found: 545 version references to update         │
│ Target version: v2.5.1                          │
│                                                 │
│ Top files affected:                             │
│   • docs/VERSION-HISTORY.md (63 changes)        │
│   • README.md (35 changes)                      │
│   • CLAUDE.md (23 changes)                      │
│   ... and 64 more files                         │
│                                                 │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Then you get a single prompt:

Apply 545 version reference updates to v2.5.1?

  A) Yes, update all
  B) Show detailed file list first
  C) Cancel - don't apply updates

docs.title: Try Other Categories
docs.text:
# Fix command counts
/craft:docs:update --category=command_counts

# Add missing help
/craft:docs:update --category=missing_help

# Fix broken links
/craft:docs:update --category=broken_links

docs.title: Preview a Specific Category
docs.text:
Combine --dry-run with --category:

/craft:docs:update --interactive --category=version_refs --dry-run

This shows what would change for just version references.

docs.title: Auto-Apply (No Prompts)
docs.text:
If you're confident, skip all prompts:

/craft:docs:update --auto-yes

⚠️ Warning: This applies ALL updates without asking!

Better approach:

# Dry-run first
/craft:docs:update --dry-run

# Then auto-apply if it looks good
/craft:docs:update --auto-yes

docs.title: Batch Update Multiple Categories
docs.text:
Update several categories at once:

# Update metadata categories
/craft:docs:update --interactive --category=version_refs
/craft:docs:update --interactive --category=command_counts

# Review changes
git diff

# Commit both
git commit -m "docs: update metadata (versions and counts)"

docs.title: Scenario 1: After Version Bump
docs.text:
You just released v2.5.1 and need to update all docs:

# Update version references
/craft:docs:update --category=version_refs --auto-yes

# Update command counts (if commands added)
/craft:docs:update --category=command_counts --auto-yes

docs.title: Scenario 2: Documentation Audit
docs.text:
You want to fix all documentation issues:

# Preview everything first
/craft:docs:update --interactive --dry-run

# Review what would change

# Apply interactively
/craft:docs:update --interactive

docs.title: Scenario 3: Specific Issue
docs.text:
You know there are broken links:

/craft:docs:update --category=broken_links

docs.title: Scenario 4: New Commands Added
docs.text:
You added commands and they need help:

/craft:docs:update --category=missing_help

docs.title: "Too Many Changes!"
docs.text:
Start with one category:

/craft:docs:update --category=version_refs

docs.title: "I'm Not Sure What Changed"
docs.text:
Always use dry-run first:

/craft:docs:update --interactive --dry-run

docs.title: "I Made a Mistake!"
docs.text:
Revert with git:

git checkout .  # Undo all changes

docs.title: "Utilities Not Found"
docs.text:
Check installation:

ls utils/docs_detector.py
ls utils/help_file_validator.py

If missing, you're on the wrong branch.

docs.title: What You've Learned
docs.text: ✅ How to preview documentation issues (dry-run) ✅ How to use interactive prompts to choose fixes ✅ How to verify changes before committing ✅ How to update specific categories ✅ How to handle common scenarios

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Try it yourself: Run /craft:docs:update --interactive --dry-run
  2. Fix real issues: Apply updates you approved
  3. Explore categories: Try category-specific mode
  4. Read the reference: See REFCARD-DOCS-UPDATE.md

Need help? Check the full documentation or run /craft:help docs:update.

docs.title: Tutorial: Interactive Orchestration
docs.text:
What you'll learn: How to use the new interactive orchestration workflow with plan confirmation, wave checkpoints, and mode selection.

Level: Intermediate

Prerequisites: Basic familiarity with /craft:orchestrate

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
1. Select an orchestration mode interactively
  2. Review and confirm a task plan before execution
  3. Use wave checkpoints to control progress
  4. Handle decision points during orchestration
  5. Choose the right mode for your task

docs.title: Step 1.1: Launch Without a Mode
docs.text:
When you run orchestrate without specifying a mode, you'll be prompted:

/craft:orchestrate "add user authentication with OAuth"

The orchestrator asks which mode to use:

? Which orchestration mode should I use?
  > Default (Recommended) — 2 agents max, balanced speed and oversight
    Debug — 1 agent, sequential, verbose output
    Optimize — 4 agents, aggressive parallelization
    Release — 4 agents, full audit trail

Select Default for most tasks. Use Debug when troubleshooting, Optimize for speed, and Release for thorough pre-release validation.

docs.title: Step 1.2: Launch With a Mode
docs.text:
Skip the mode prompt by specifying it directly:

/craft:orchestrate "add user authentication" optimize

This jumps straight to the task analysis.

docs.title: Step 2.1: Read the Task Analysis
docs.text:
After mode selection, the orchestrator analyzes your task and shows a plan:

## TASK ANALYSIS

Request: Add user authentication with OAuth
Complexity: complex
Mode: default (2 agents max)
Estimated subtasks: 5
Delegation strategy: hybrid

| # | Task              | Agent | Wave | Dependencies |
|---|-------------------|-------|------|--------------|
| 1 | Design auth flow  | arch  | 1    | none         |
| 2 | Implement backend | code  | 1    | none         |
| 3 | Create login UI   | code  | 2    | 1            |
| 4 | Add tests         | test  | 2    | 2            |
| 5 | Update docs       | doc   | 3    | 2,3          |

Key things to check:

  • Are the subtasks correct? Missing anything?
  • Are the wave groupings logical? (Independent tasks in the same wave)
  • Are dependencies accurate?

docs.title: Step 2.2: Confirm or Modify
docs.text:
? Proceed with this orchestration plan?
  > Yes - Start Wave 1 (Recommended)
    Modify steps
    Change mode
    Cancel
Choice When to use Yes - Start Wave 1 Plan looks good, proceed Modify steps Need to add, remove, or reorder tasks Change mode Realize you need a different execution style Cancel Task isn't right, start over

docs.title: Step 3.1: Wave 1 Executes
docs.text:
After confirmation, the orchestrator spawns agents for Wave 1:

Spawning Wave 1 agents...
  arch-1: Design auth flow (running)
  code-1: Implement backend (running)

docs.title: Step 3.2: Wave Checkpoint
docs.text:
When Wave 1 completes, you see results and are asked to continue:

## WAVE 1 COMPLETE

| Agent  | Task            | Status  | Output                        |
|--------|-----------------|---------|-------------------------------|
| arch-1 | Design auth     | Done    | OAuth 2.0 + PKCE recommended  |
| code-1 | Backend routes  | Done    | 4 endpoints created           |

Next: Wave 2
| Agent  | Task            | Dependencies |
|--------|-----------------|-------------|
| code-2 | Create login UI | arch-1      |
| test-1 | Add tests       | code-1      |

? Wave 1 complete. Continue to Wave 2?
  > Yes - Continue (Recommended)
    Review results first
    Modify next wave
    Stop here
Choice When to use Yes - Continue Results look good, proceed to next wave Review results first Want to see detailed output from Wave 1 agents Modify next wave Need to adjust Wave 2 tasks based on Wave 1 results Stop here Wave 1 output is sufficient, don't need more waves

docs.title: Step 3.3: Continue Through Waves
docs.text: Repeat for each wave until all tasks are complete. The final summary shows everything that was accomplished.

docs.title: Step 4.1: Active Prompts
docs.text:
During orchestration, if a decision is needed (e.g., a design choice), you'll get a structured prompt instead of a passive text listing:

## DECISION REQUIRED

Context: The auth implementation needs a token strategy
Impact: Affects security model and scalability
Blocking: code-1 agent paused waiting

? The auth implementation needs a token strategy. Which approach?
  > JWT with refresh tokens (Recommended) — Stateless, scalable
    Session cookies — Simple, easy revocation
    Hybrid (JWT + sessions) — Most flexible but complex

Select an option and the blocked agent resumes immediately.

docs.title: When to Use Each Mode
docs.text: Scenario Mode Why Add a feature default Balanced — 2 agents, asks per wave Debug a failing test debug Sequential — 1 agent, asks every step, verbose output Bulk refactoring optimize Fast — 4 agents, minimal prompts Pre-release validation release Thorough — 4 agents, asks every step, full audit

docs.title: Output Differences
docs.text:
Try the same task in different modes to see the difference:

# Minimal output, per-wave checkpoints
/craft:orchestrate "add auth" default

# Verbose output, per-step checkpoints
/craft:orchestrate "add auth" debug

# Parallel execution, wave-end checkpoints
/craft:orchestrate "add auth" optimize

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
You've learned how to:

  • Select orchestration modes (interactively or via argument)
  • Review task plans before any agents are spawned
  • Use wave checkpoints to control execution flow
  • Respond to structured decision points
  • Choose the right mode for your task

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Deep dive: Orchestrator Deep Dive — Full reference for all orchestrator features
  • Pattern guide: Interactive Commands Guide — How this pattern works across all 4 commands
  • Check command: /craft:check — Uses the same interactive pattern for pre-flight checks

docs.title: Tutorial: Orchestrator Modes Compared
docs.text:
Learn when to use each orchestrator mode — See the same task executed in all 4 modes with performance metrics, output comparisons, and decision guidance.

Level: Intermediate Time: 20-30 minutes Prerequisites: Basic understanding of /craft:orchestrate

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
1. How the same task executes differently across 4 modes
  2. Performance and token usage trade-offs
  3. When to use each mode
  4. How to read orchestrator output
  5. Mode selection decision trees

docs.title: The Reference Task
docs.text:
We'll execute this task in all 4 modes to compare behavior:

Task: "Implement user authentication with OAuth 2.0"

Expected complexity:

  • 5 subtasks (design, backend, frontend, tests, docs)
  • Estimated time: 8-15 minutes depending on mode
  • Involves: architecture decisions, code implementation, test generation

docs.text: /craft:orchestrate "implement user authentication with OAuth 2.0" default

docs.title: When to Use Default Mode
docs.text:
• ✅ Most tasks (balanced speed and oversight)
  • ✅ Unknown complexity (mode auto-adjusts)
  • ✅ Want checkpoints between major phases
  • ✅ Budget-conscious (moderate token usage)

docs.title: When to Use Debug Mode
docs.text:
• ✅ Debugging a failing orchestration
  • ✅ Understanding how decisions are made
  • ✅ Learning orchestrator behavior
  • ✅ Troubleshooting agent coordination issues
  • ❌ Time-sensitive tasks (slowest mode)
  • ❌ Simple tasks (overkill)
